(I) $\mathrm{Br}_{2}$
(II) dil. $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4} / \mathrm{OH}^{-}$
(III) $\mathrm{OsO}_{4} / \mathrm{NaHSO}_{3} / \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$
(IV) $\mathrm{H}_{2} / \mathrm{Ni} / \Delta$
options: (IV) only
(I) only
(II) and (III) only
(II), (III) and (IV)
questions.solution:
Addition of $\mathrm{Br}_{2}$ to alkenes proceeds via a cyclic bromonium ion intermediate, resulting in anti addition.

Addition of cold dilute alkaline $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$ proceeds via a cyclic manganate ester intermediate, resulting in syn dihydroxylation.

Addition of $\mathrm{OsO}_{4}$ followed by hydrolysis proceeds via a cyclic osmate ester intermediate, resulting in syn dihydroxylation.

Catalytic hydrogenation using $\mathrm{H}_{2} / \mathrm{Ni}$ involves the adsorption of hydrogen on the metal surface, delivering both hydrogen atoms to the same face of the alkene, resulting in syn addition.

Thus, reagents (II), (III), and (IV) undergo syn addition.

Answer: (II), (III) and (IV)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: During the free-radical chlorination of n-pentane, assuming no regioselectivity (i.e., all hydrogen atoms are equally reactive), what is the expected molar ratio of 1-chloropentane, 2-chloropentane, and 3-chloropentane formed, respectively?
questions.solution:
The structure of n-pentane is $CH_3-CH_2-CH_2-CH_2-CH_3$.

The formation of different monochlorinated products depends on the number of equivalent hydrogen atoms available for substitution, assuming all hydrogen atoms are equally reactive.

1-chloropentane is formed by the substitution of any of the $6$ primary hydrogen atoms present at the $C_1$ and $C_5$ positions.

2-chloropentane is formed by the substitution of any of the $4$ secondary hydrogen atoms present at the $C_2$ and $C_4$ positions.

3-chloropentane is formed by the substitution of any of the $2$ secondary hydrogen atoms present at the $C_3$ position.

The expected molar ratio of 1-chloropentane, 2-chloropentane, and 3-chloropentane is equal to the ratio of the number of available hydrogen atoms for each product.

Ratio $= 6 : 4 : 2$

Simplifying the ratio, we get $3 : 2 : 1$.

Answer: $3:2:1$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Which of the following compounds will undergo electrophilic addition reactions?
(I) $\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$
(II) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{C}\equiv\mathrm{CH}$
(III) $\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$
(IV)
options: (I), (II) and (IV)
(I), (II) and (III)
(I) and (II)
(I), (II), (III) and (IV)
questions.solution:
Electrophilic addition reactions are characteristic of unsaturated hydrocarbons like alkenes and alkynes, where an electrophile attacks the electron-rich $\pi$ bond.

Compound (I) is ethene ($\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$), which readily undergoes electrophilic addition.

Compound (II) is propyne ($\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{C}\equiv\mathrm{CH}$), an alkyne, which also undergoes electrophilic addition.

Compound (III) is 1,3-butadiene ($\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$), a conjugated diene, which undergoes electrophilic addition to yield 1,2- and 1,4-addition products.

Compound (IV) is tetranitroethene. The four $-\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ groups are strongly electron-withdrawing due to their $-I$ and $-M$ effects. They drastically reduce the electron density on the $\mathrm{C=C}$ double bond, making it highly electron-deficient. As a result, it does not undergo electrophilic addition; instead, it is highly susceptible to nucleophilic addition.

Therefore, only compounds (I), (II), and (III) will undergo electrophilic addition reactions.

Answer: (I), (II) and (III)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following compounds in decreasing order of their reactivity towards electrophilic addition reactions:

(i) $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$
(ii)
(iii)
(iv) $\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{NO}_{2}$
options: (ii) > (iii) > (i) > (iv)
(iv) > (i) > (ii) > (iii)
(ii) > (iii) > (iv) > (i)
(iii) > (ii) > (i) > (iv)
questions.solution:
The reactivity of alkenes towards electrophilic addition reactions depends on the electron density of the carbon-carbon double bond and the stability of the intermediate carbocation formed during the rate-determining step.

In compound (iv), the $-\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ group is strongly electron-withdrawing due to its $-I$ and $-R$ effects. This drastically reduces the electron density on the double bond and highly destabilizes the intermediate carbocation, making it the least reactive.

For the remaining compounds, the addition of an electrophile (like $H^{+}$) yields the following carbocations:
(i) $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}-\mathrm{C}^{+}H-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ (Secondary benzylic carbocation)
(ii) $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}-\mathrm{C}^{+}(\mathrm{CH}_{3})-CH_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ (Tertiary benzylic carbocation)
(iii) $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}-\mathrm{C}^{+}(\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5})-CH_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ (Tertiary benzylic carbocation)

Tertiary carbocations are generally more stable than secondary carbocations, so (ii) and (iii) are more reactive than (i).

Comparing (ii) and (iii): In compound (iii), the presence of two bulky phenyl groups on the same carbon causes steric hindrance, preventing both rings from being simultaneously coplanar with the empty p-orbital of the carbocation. This steric inhibition of resonance reduces the expected stabilization. Furthermore, the phenyl group has a $-I$ effect, which decreases the initial electron density of the alkene compared to the electron-donating methyl group ($+I$ and hyperconjugation) in compound (ii). Thus, the carbocation formed from (ii) is more stable and forms faster than that from (iii).

Therefore, the decreasing order of reactivity is (ii) > (iii) > (i) > (iv).

Answer: (ii) > (iii) > (i) > (iv)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the alkene which upon reaction with $\mathrm{Br}_2$ yields the given 50:50 mixture of enantiomers.
questions.solution:
The addition of bromine ($\text{Br}_2$) to an alkene is a stereospecific reaction that proceeds via a cyclic bromonium ion intermediate, resulting in an anti addition.

Let us analyze the stereochemistry of the given products. The products are drawn as Fischer projections. In these projections, the two bromine atoms are on the same side of the carbon chain, and the two hydrogen atoms are also on the same side. This relative stereochemical arrangement is known as the erythro configuration. Since the molecule is asymmetric (the ends are a methyl group and an ethyl group), the erythro form is chiral and exists as a 50:50 racemic mixture of enantiomers, which is exactly what is shown in the reaction.

We can determine the stereochemistry of the starting alkene using standard stereochemical rules for addition reactions:
1. Cis alkene + Anti addition $\rightarrow$ Threo product (racemic mixture)
2. Trans alkene + Anti addition $\rightarrow$ Erythro product (meso or racemic mixture depending on symmetry)

Since the given products have the erythro configuration, the starting alkene must have a trans configuration.

Now let us examine the given options:
(A) The methyl and ethyl groups are on opposite sides of the double bond, making it trans-2-pentene.
(B) The methyl and ethyl groups are on the same side of the double bond, making it cis-2-pentene.
(C) This is 2-methyl-1-butene, a terminal alkene, which would not give 2,3-dibromopentane.
(D) The methyl and ethyl groups are on the same side, making it cis-2-pentene.

Therefore, the alkene that yields the given erythro mixture of enantiomers upon anti addition of $\text{Br}_2$ is trans-2-pentene.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: When isobutane is brominated in the presence of diffused sunlight, the product formed is:
options: exclusively $n$-butyl bromide
$sec$-butyl bromide
exclusively tertiary butyl bromide
exclusively isobutyl bromide
questions.solution:
Isobutane ($2$-methylpropane) contains $9$ primary hydrogen atoms and $1$ tertiary hydrogen atom.

Bromination of alkanes is a highly selective free radical substitution reaction. The relative rates of abstraction of primary, secondary, and tertiary hydrogen atoms by bromine radicals at room temperature are approximately $1 : 82 : 1600$.

The ratio of primary to tertiary products formed is $(9 \times 1) : (1 \times 1600) = 9 : 1600$.

Since the rate of abstraction of the tertiary hydrogen is extremely high compared to primary hydrogens, the product formed is almost exclusively tertiary butyl bromide.

Answer: exclusively tertiary butyl bromide

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the reagent required to carry out the following transformation.
options: $\mathrm{Br}_{2}$
HBr
HCl
NaBr
questions.solution:
The given reaction is an electrophilic addition reaction of an alkene.

The reactant is 2-methylpent-1-ene and the product is 2-bromo-2-methylpentane.

The addition of hydrogen bromide (HBr) to an unsymmetrical alkene follows Markovnikov's rule. The proton ($\mathrm{H}^{+}$) adds to the less substituted carbon atom of the double bond (C1) to form a more stable tertiary carbocation intermediate at C2.

The bromide ion ($\mathrm{Br}^{-}$) then attacks the tertiary carbocation to yield the Markovnikov product, 2-bromo-2-methylpentane.

Therefore, the required reagent is HBr.

Answer: HBr

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following reaction sequences represents the best synthesis of (E)-3-hexene?
questions.solution:
The target molecule is (E)-3-hexene, which is a trans-alkene with a six-carbon chain and a double bond at the 3-position.

Let us evaluate the given reaction sequences:

In option (A), 2-butanone reacts with ethyl lithium to form 3-methylpentan-3-ol. Reaction with HBr gives 3-bromo-3-methylpentane. Dehydrohalogenation yields 3-methyl-2-pentene as the major product, not 3-hexene.

In option (B), ethynyl anion ($HC\equiv C^-$) reacts with 1-bromopropane to form 1-pentyne. Deprotonation with NaH followed by alkylation with methyl bromide gives 2-hexyne. Reduction with $Na/NH_3$ yields (E)-2-hexene, not (E)-3-hexene.

In option (C), ethynyl anion reacts with ethyl bromide to form 1-butyne. Deprotonation with NaH followed by alkylation with another equivalent of ethyl bromide gives 3-hexyne. Finally, Birch reduction of 3-hexyne using $Na/NH_3$ selectively produces the trans-alkene, (E)-3-hexene. This matches our target molecule perfectly.

In option (D), propanal reacts with ethyl lithium to form pentan-3-ol. Reaction with HBr gives 3-bromopentane. Elimination using a bulky base (t-butoxide) yields 2-pentene, not 3-hexene.

Thus, the sequence in option (C) represents the best synthesis of (E)-3-hexene.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the starting material that yields the given product upon the following reaction sequence:

Starting Material $\xrightarrow{\begin{array}{l}\text{(i) } \mathrm{O}_{3}\text{, then } \mathrm{Zn}\text{, } \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O} \\ \text{(ii) } \mathrm{NaBH}_{4} \\ \text{(iii) Excess } \mathrm{PBr}_{3} \\ \text{(iv) Excess KCN} \\ \text{(v) } \mathrm{H}^{+}\text{, } \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}\text{, } \Delta\end{array}}$
questions.solution:
The final product is 2-methylheptanedioic acid, $\mathrm{HOOC-CH(CH_3)-CH_2-CH_2-CH_2-CH_2-COOH}$.

The hydrolysis of nitriles in step (v) produces the dicarboxylic acid. The intermediate before this step is 2-methylheptanedinitrile, $\mathrm{NC-CH(CH_3)-CH_2-CH_2-CH_2-CH_2-CN}$.

The reaction with excess $\mathrm{KCN}$ in step (iv) substitutes bromide ions with cyanide ions. The intermediate before this step is 1,5-dibromohexane, $\mathrm{CH_3-CH(Br)-CH_2-CH_2-CH_2-CH_2-Br}$.

The reaction with excess $\mathrm{PBr_3}$ in step (iii) converts alcohols to bromides. The intermediate before this step is hexane-1,5-diol, $\mathrm{CH_3-CH(OH)-CH_2-CH_2-CH_2-CH_2-OH}$.

The reduction with $\mathrm{NaBH_4}$ in step (ii) produces alcohols from carbonyl compounds. The intermediate before this step is 5-oxohexanal, $\mathrm{CH_3-C(=O)-CH_2-CH_2-CH_2-CHO}$.

The reductive ozonolysis in step (i) cleaves the carbon-carbon double bond to form carbonyl compounds. To produce 5-oxohexanal, the starting material must be a five-membered ring with a methyl group on one of the double-bonded carbons, which is 1-methylcyclopentene.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the propagation step(s) in the following free radical chain reaction.

Overall Reaction:


Steps:
(I)
(II) $n\text{-}\mathrm{Bu}_{3}\mathrm{Sn}^{\bullet} +$
(III)
options: (III) only
Both (I) and (II)
(II) only
(I) only
questions.solution:
A free radical chain reaction consists of initiation, propagation, and termination steps.

Step (I) involves the combination of two cyclopentyl radicals to form a neutral molecule (dicyclopentyl). Since two radicals are consumed to form a stable product, this is a termination step.

Step (II) involves the reaction of a radical ($n\text{-}\mathrm{Bu}_{3}\mathrm{Sn}^{\bullet}$) with a neutral molecule (cyclopentyl bromide) to generate a new neutral molecule ($n\text{-}\mathrm{Bu}_{3}\mathrm{Sn}\text{-}\mathrm{Br}$) and a new radical (cyclopentyl radical). Since a radical is consumed and another is produced, allowing the chain to continue, this is a propagation step.

Step (III) involves the reaction of an initiator radical ($\cdot\mathrm{In}$) with a neutral molecule ($n\text{-}\mathrm{Bu}_{3}\mathrm{Sn}\text{-}\mathrm{H}$) to generate the first chain-carrying radical ($n\text{-}\mathrm{Bu}_{3}\mathrm{Sn}^{\bullet}$). This is an initiation step.

Therefore, the only propagation step among the given options is (II).

Answer: (II) only

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following synthetic schemes is the best for the synthesis of 2-bromo-1-chloro-4-nitrobenzene?
questions.solution:
The target molecule is 2-bromo-1-chloro-4-nitrobenzene. In this structure, the $\text{Cl}$ and $\text{NO}_2$ groups are para to each other, while the $\text{Br}$ group is ortho to $\text{Cl}$ and meta to $\text{NO}_2$.

To achieve this specific substitution pattern, the directing effects of each group must be considered:

First, chlorination of benzene yields chlorobenzene. The $\text{Cl}$ atom is an ortho/para directing group.

Second, nitration of chlorobenzene yields 1-chloro-4-nitrobenzene as the major product, as the para position is less sterically hindered.

Third, in 1-chloro-4-nitrobenzene, both groups synergistically direct the next electrophilic aromatic substitution to the same position. The $\text{Cl}$ group directs ortho to itself, and the $\text{NO}_2$ group directs meta to itself.

Finally, bromination of 1-chloro-4-nitrobenzene will regioselectively occur at this position, yielding 2-bromo-1-chloro-4-nitrobenzene.

This sequence corresponds to the reagents: $\text{Cl}_2/\text{AlCl}_3$ followed by $\text{HNO}_3/\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ and then $\text{Br}_2/\text{FeBr}_3$.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product $\mathrm{P}$ in the following reaction:
options: $\mathrm{CH_2=CH-CH=CH_2}$
No reaction
$\mathrm{CH_2=C=CH-CH_3}$
$\mathrm{CH_3CH_2C \equiv CH}$
questions.solution:
The reaction involves the base-catalyzed isomerization of an internal alkyne to a terminal alkyne upon heating with a strong base like sodamide ($\text{NaNH}_2$).

The strong base abstracts a proton from the methyl group adjacent to the triple bond, causing the triple bond to migrate towards the end of the chain via an allene intermediate.

This migration is thermodynamically driven by the formation of a highly stable sodium acetylide salt, as terminal alkynes possess a more acidic proton compared to internal alkynes.

Therefore, the internal alkyne but-2-yne ($\text{CH}_3\text{C}\equiv\text{CCH}_3$) isomerizes to the terminal alkyne but-1-yne ($\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{C}\equiv\text{CH}$).

Answer : $\mathrm{CH_3CH_2C \equiv CH}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: When ethylbenzene is treated with chlorine in the presence of light, the major product formed is:
options: $\beta$-phenylethyl chloride
$o$-chloroethylbenzene
$\alpha$-phenylethyl chloride
a mixture of $o$- and $p$-chloroethylbenzene
questions.solution:
The reaction of ethylbenzene with chlorine in the presence of light proceeds via a free radical substitution mechanism at the alkyl side chain.

The abstraction of a hydrogen atom from the $\alpha$-carbon yields the $\alpha$-free radical, $C_6H_5-\dot{C}H-CH_3$. This radical is highly stabilized by resonance with the adjacent benzene ring.

The abstraction of a hydrogen atom from the $\beta$-carbon yields the $\beta$-free radical, $C_6H_5-CH_2-\dot{C}H_2$, which is a primary radical and lacks resonance stabilization.

Due to the greater stability of the benzylic $\alpha$-radical, the major product formed is $\alpha$-phenylethyl chloride, $C_6H_5-CHCl-CH_3$.

Answer: $\alpha$-phenylethyl chloride

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following sequence of reactions:
questions.solution:
The first step involves the dissolving metal reduction of 1-phenylpropyne using $\text{Na}$ in liquid $\text{NH}_3$, which reduces the internal alkyne to a trans-alkene.

$\text{C}_6\text{H}_5-\text{C}\equiv\text{C}-\text{CH}_3 \xrightarrow{\text{Na, NH}_3} \text{C}_6\text{H}_5-\text{CH}=\text{CH}-\text{CH}_3$

The second step is the electrophilic addition of $\text{HBr}$ to the resulting 1-phenylpropene. Protonation of the double bond occurs to form the most stable carbocation intermediate.

Protonation at the $\beta$-carbon yields a resonance-stabilized benzylic carbocation ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_5-\text{CH}^+-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_3$), whereas protonation at the $\alpha$-carbon yields a less stable secondary carbocation ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_5-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}^+-\text{CH}_3$).

The reaction proceeds via the more stable benzylic carbocation, followed by the nucleophilic attack of the bromide ion ($\text{Br}^-$) to form 1-bromo-1-phenylpropane.

$\text{C}_6\text{H}_5-\text{CH}^+-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_3 + \text{Br}^- \rightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_5-\text{CH}(\text{Br})-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_3$

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following sequence of reactions:
questions.solution:
Step 1: Reaction (i) is a Friedel-Crafts acylation. Benzene reacts with acetic anhydride in the presence of anhydrous $\text{AlCl}_3$ to form acetophenone.
$\text{C}_6\text{H}_6 + (\text{CH}_3\text{CO})_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{AlCl}_3} \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COCH}_3$

Step 2: Reaction (ii) is a Clemmensen reduction. The carbonyl group of acetophenone is reduced to a methylene group by zinc amalgam ($\text{Zn/Hg}$) and concentrated $\text{HCl}$, yielding ethylbenzene.
$\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COCH}_3 \xrightarrow{\text{Zn/Hg, HCl}} \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_3$

Step 3: Reaction (iii) is a Friedel-Crafts alkylation. Ethylbenzene reacts with methyl chloride ($\text{CH}_3\text{Cl}$) in the presence of anhydrous $\text{AlCl}_3$. The ethyl group is an activating and ortho/para-directing group. Due to steric hindrance at the ortho position, the para isomer is formed as the major product.
$\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_3 + \text{CH}_3\text{Cl} \xrightarrow{\text{AlCl}_3} \text{1-ethyl-4-methylbenzene (major)}$

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the following statements regarding addition reactions of conjugated dienes:

(I) A conjugated diene undergoes both direct (1,2-) as well as conjugate (1,4-) addition.
(II) A conjugated diene undergoes only direct addition.
(III) A conjugated diene undergoes only conjugate addition.
(IV) The thermodynamically controlled product is obtained via the less stable reaction intermediate.

Which of the given statements is/are correct?
options: Only (I)
(I) and (IV)
Only (III)
(II) and (III)
questions.solution:
Conjugated dienes undergo electrophilic addition reactions to yield a mixture of two products: the 1,2-addition product (direct addition) and the 1,4-addition product (conjugate addition). Thus, statement (I) is correct, while statements (II) and (III) are incorrect.

The addition proceeds via a common resonance-stabilized allylic carbocation intermediate. The kinetically controlled product (usually the 1,2-product) forms faster due to the proximity of the nucleophile to the site of initial electrophilic attack. The thermodynamically controlled product (usually the 1,4-product) is the more stable alkene (more substituted double bond) and dominates at higher temperatures. Since both products arise from the same intermediate, statement (IV) is incorrect.

Therefore, only statement (I) is correct.

Answer: Only (I)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following sequence of reactions:
questions.solution:
The given starting material is 1-pentene.

In the first step, the addition of $HBr$ in the dark and under an $N_2$ atmosphere ensures the absence of peroxides, leading to an electrophilic addition that follows Markovnikov's rule. Protonation of 1-pentene forms the more stable secondary carbocation (2-pentyl cation), which is then attacked by the bromide ion to yield 2-bromopentane as the major product.

In the second step, 2-bromopentane undergoes an $S_N2$ reaction with triphenylphosphine ($Ph_3P$) to form the corresponding phosphonium salt, 2-pentyltriphenylphosphonium bromide. Subsequent treatment with the strong base n-butyllithium (nBuLi) deprotonates the acidic $\alpha$-carbon to generate the phosphorus ylide, 2-pentylidenetriphenylphosphorane.

In the final step, the ylide reacts with cyclohexanone in a Wittig reaction. The nucleophilic carbon of the ylide attacks the carbonyl carbon, forming an oxaphosphetane intermediate that eliminates triphenylphosphine oxide. This results in the formation of an exocyclic double bond, yielding (pentan-2-ylidene)cyclohexane.

The final major product has a methyl group and a propyl group attached to the exocyclic double bond carbon, which corresponds to the structure in Option (B).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the following reaction sequence:
questions.solution:
Reaction of 1-phenylpropyne with $Na$ in liquid $NH_3$ (Birch reduction) reduces the internal alkyne to a trans-alkene.

$Ph-C \equiv C-CH_3 \xrightarrow{Na, NH_3} Ph-CH=CH-CH_3$

Addition of $HBr$ to the alkene proceeds via an electrophilic addition mechanism. Protonation of the double bond forms the more stable carbocation intermediate. The possible carbocations are:

1. $Ph-\overset{+}{C}H-CH_2-CH_3$ (Benzylic carbocation, highly stabilized by resonance)
2. $Ph-CH_2-\overset{+}{C}H-CH_3$ (Secondary carbocation, less stable)

The resonance-stabilized benzylic carbocation is formed preferentially. Attack by the bromide ion ($Br^-$) yields 1-bromo-1-phenylpropane.

$Ph-\overset{+}{C}H-CH_2-CH_3 + Br^- \rightarrow Ph-CH(Br)-CH_2-CH_3$

This structure corresponds to option (D).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: When phenyl ethanoate is alkylated with chloroethane in the presence of $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$, the para product is formed as the major product. This is primarily because:
options: the $-\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ group is a weakly activating group and an ortho/para director.
the $-\mathrm{Cl}$ group is a weakly deactivating group and an ortho/para director.
the $-\mathrm{OCOCH}_{3}$ group is an activating group and an ortho/para director.
alkylation usually occurs at the para position.
questions.solution:
Phenyl ethanoate has the structure $C_6H_5-O-CO-CH_3$.

The group attached to the benzene ring is $-O-CO-CH_3$.

The oxygen atom directly attached to the benzene ring has lone pairs of electrons, which it can donate to the ring through resonance (+R effect).

This increases the electron density on the benzene ring, making the $-O-CO-CH_3$ group an activating group.

The electron density increases specifically at the ortho and para positions, making it an ortho/para directing group.

During Friedel-Crafts alkylation with chloroethane and $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$, the electrophile $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}^{+}$ attacks the ortho and para positions.

Due to steric hindrance caused by the bulky $-O-CO-CH_3$ group at the ortho position, the para product is formed as the major product.

Answer: the $-\mathrm{OCOCH}_{3}$ group is an activating group and an ortho/para director.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Choose the incorrect statement about the following acid-base reactions involving propyne. The $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}$ values are given as: $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O} = 16$, $\mathrm{H}_{3}\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C}\equiv\mathrm{CH} = 25$, and $\mathrm{NH}_{3} = 34$.

(i) $\mathrm{H}_{3}\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C}\equiv\mathrm{CH} + \mathrm{OH}^{-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_{3}\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C}\equiv\mathrm{C}^{-} + \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$
(ii) $\mathrm{H}_{3}\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C}\equiv\mathrm{CH} + \mathrm{NH}_{2}^{-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_{3}\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C}\equiv\mathrm{C}^{-} + \mathrm{NH}_{3}$
options: The equilibrium in equation (ii) lies to the right.
The equilibrium in equation (i) lies to the left.
Propyne ($\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{C}\equiv\mathrm{CH}$) is a stronger acid than ammonia ($\mathrm{NH}_{3}$).
In reaction (ii), ammonia ($\mathrm{NH}_{3}$) acts as a base.
questions.solution:
In an acid-base reaction, the equilibrium favors the formation of the weaker acid and weaker base.

For reaction (i), the acid on the reactant side is propyne ($\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}} = 25$) and the conjugate acid on the product side is $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$ ($\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}} = 16$). Since $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$ is a stronger acid than propyne, the equilibrium lies to the left.

For reaction (ii), the acid on the reactant side is propyne ($\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}} = 25$) and the conjugate acid on the product side is $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ ($\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}} = 34$). Since propyne is a stronger acid than $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$, the equilibrium lies to the right.

Comparing $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}$ values, propyne ($\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}} = 25$) is a stronger acid than ammonia ($\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}} = 34$).

In reaction (ii), $\mathrm{NH}_{2}^{-}$ accepts a proton from propyne to form $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$. Therefore, $\mathrm{NH}_{2}^{-}$ acts as a base, and $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ is its conjugate acid. In the reverse reaction, $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ would donate a proton, acting as an acid. Thus, the statement that ammonia acts as a base in reaction (ii) is incorrect.

Answer: In reaction (ii), ammonia ($\mathrm{NH}_{3}$) acts as a base.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the most appropriate reagent for the following conversion:
options: All of these
Red $\mathrm{P} + \mathrm{I}_{2}$
$\mathrm{Zn-Hg} / \mathrm{HCl}$
$\mathrm{NH}_{2}\mathrm{NH}_{2} ; \mathrm{EtOH} / \mathrm{OH}^{-}$
questions.solution:
The given conversion requires the reduction of a ketone ($>\text{C=O}$) to a methylene group ($>\text{CH}_2$) without affecting the secondary alkyl bromide ($-\text{Br}$) group.

Let us evaluate the given reagents:

1. Wolff-Kishner reduction ($\text{NH}_2\text{NH}_2 ; \text{EtOH} / \text{OH}^-$): This reaction takes place in strongly basic conditions. Under these conditions, the secondary alkyl bromide will undergo dehydrohalogenation (elimination) to form an alkene. Thus, it is not suitable.

2. Red $\text{P} + \text{I}_2$ (or Red $\text{P} + \text{HI}$): This is a very strong reducing agent. It will reduce both the carbonyl group and the alkyl bromide, yielding cyclohexane as the final product. Thus, it is not suitable.

3. Clemmensen reduction ($\text{Zn-Hg} / \text{HCl}$): This reaction takes place in acidic conditions. Alkyl halides are generally stable to these acidic conditions and do not undergo elimination or substitution. Therefore, the ketone is selectively reduced to an alkane while the bromine atom remains unaffected.

Therefore, Clemmensen reduction is the most appropriate method for this conversion.

Answer: $\mathrm{Zn-Hg} / \mathrm{HCl}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
When 1-phenylpropyne reacts with $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$ in the presence of $\mathrm{HgSO}_{4}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}$, the major product is propiophenone, as shown below:


Which of the following statements best explains the regioselectivity of this reaction?
options: Alkyl groups are weak electron donors due to inductive effects and hyperconjugation.
Carbonyl groups are electron-withdrawing due to resonance.
The phenyl group can stabilise the intermediate positive charge by resonance.
The reaction is controlled by steric factors.
questions.solution:
The reaction of 1-phenylpropyne ($Ph-C \equiv C-CH_3$) with $H_2O$ in the presence of $HgSO_4$ and $H_2SO_4$ is an electrophilic addition reaction. The regioselectivity is determined by the stability of the intermediate carbocation.

Protonation (or mercuration) of the alkyne can lead to two possible intermediate vinyl cations:
1. $Ph-C^+=CH-CH_3$
2. $Ph-CH=C^+-CH_3$

The first carbocation is highly stabilised by resonance with the adjacent phenyl ring, as the positive charge can be delocalised over the aromatic system. The second carbocation is only stabilised by the weaker hyperconjugation and inductive effects of the methyl group.

Because the resonance stabilisation provided by the phenyl group is much stronger, the reaction proceeds predominantly via the first intermediate. Water attacks the positively charged carbon adjacent to the phenyl ring, forming an enol ($Ph-C(OH)=CH-CH_3$), which then tautomerises to form propiophenone ($Ph-C(=O)-CH_2-CH_3$).

Therefore, the regioselectivity is best explained by the phenyl group's ability to stabilise the intermediate positive charge through resonance.

Answer: The phenyl group can stabilise the intermediate positive charge by resonance.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The reaction involves an anionic oxy-Cope rearrangement. The starting material is a 1,5-diene system with an allylic alcohol.

First, the strong base KH deprotonates the hydroxyl group at C3 to form an alkoxide. The 18-crown-6 ether solvates the potassium ion, enhancing the reactivity of the alkoxide.

The substrate contains a 1,5-diene system formed by the vinyl group at C3 and the isopropenyl group at C4. The numbering for the Cope rearrangement is as follows:
1,2: The vinyl group ($-CH=CH_2$)
3: The carbon with the alkoxide ($-C(O^-)-$)
4: The adjacent ring carbon ($-CH-$)
5,6: The isopropenyl group ($-C(CH_3)=CH_2$)

The anionic oxy-Cope rearrangement proceeds via a [3,3]-sigmatropic shift. The C3-C4 bond of the six-membered ring breaks, and a new bond forms between the terminal carbons of the vinyl and isopropenyl groups (C1 and C6). This expands the 6-membered ring by 4 carbons, resulting in a 10-membered ring.

Simultaneously, the double bonds shift: the vinyl double bond moves to form an enolate at C3, and the isopropenyl double bond moves to the C4-C5 position.

The resulting enolate tautomerizes to a ketone. Tracing the atoms in the newly formed 10-membered ring:
- The original C3 becomes a ketone carbonyl ($-C(=O)-$)
- The original ring double bond remains intact as $-CH=C(CH_3)-$
- The two $CH_2$ groups of the original ring remain as $-CH_2-CH_2-$
- The new double bond from the isopropenyl group is $-CH=C(CH_3)-$
- The newly formed bond connects the remaining three $CH_2$ groups: $-CH_2-CH_2-CH_2-$

This sequence corresponds to the structure of germacrone, which is a 10-membered ring with two $(E)$-double bonds and a ketone. The correct structure matching this connectivity is given in Option (A).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product $\mathrm{P}$ in the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The reaction involves the Friedel-Crafts formylation of an aromatic compound.

Formyl fluoride ($\text{F}-\text{CHO}$) reacts with the Lewis acid $\text{BF}_3$ to generate the formyl cation ($\text{CHO}^+$), which acts as the electrophile.

The starting material, 2-ethoxy-1,3-dimethylbenzene, possesses a strongly activating ethoxy group ($-\text{OCH}_2\text{CH}_3$) and two weakly activating methyl groups ($-\text{CH}_3$). The regioselectivity of the electrophilic aromatic substitution is controlled by the stronger $+M$ activating group, which is the ethoxy group.

The ethoxy group directs the incoming electrophile to its ortho and para positions. Since both ortho positions are already occupied by methyl groups, the electrophilic attack occurs exclusively at the available para position.

Substitution of the formyl group at the para position relative to the ethoxy group yields 4-ethoxy-3,5-dimethylbenzaldehyde as the major product.

This structure corresponds to option (2).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following compounds in decreasing order of their reactivity towards electrophilic aromatic nitration:

(i)
(ii)
(iii)
(iv)
options: (i) > (ii) > (iii) > (iv)
(ii) > (iv) > (iii) > (i)
(iv) > (ii) > (i) > (iii)
(ii) > (iv) > (i) > (iii)
questions.solution:
Reactivity towards electrophilic aromatic nitration is directly proportional to the electron density on the benzene ring. Electron-donating groups increase the electron density and activate the ring, while electron-withdrawing groups decrease the electron density and deactivate the ring.

In compound (ii), the benzene ring is attached to two $-OCH_3$ groups. Both are strong electron-donating groups due to their $+M$ effect, making the ring highly activated and the most reactive.

In compound (iv), the ring has an $-NHCOCH_3$ group and an $-N(CH_3)(NO)$ group. The $-NHCOCH_3$ group is moderately activating ($+M$ effect), while the $-N(CH_3)(NO)$ group is deactivating. The presence of an activating group makes it more reactive than compounds with only deactivating groups, but less reactive than compound (ii).

In compound (iii), the ring has a $-Br$ group and a $-COCH_3$ group. $-Br$ is weakly deactivating ($-I > +M$) and $-COCH_3$ is strongly deactivating ($-M$, $-I$).

In compound (i), the ring has a $-CHO$ group and a $-COCH_3$ group. Both are strongly deactivating groups ($-M$, $-I$). Since $-CHO$ is a stronger deactivating group than $-Br$, compound (i) is less reactive than compound (iii).

Therefore, the decreasing order of reactivity is (ii) > (iv) > (iii) > (i).

Answer: (ii) > (iv) > (iii) > (i)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed upon the nitration of the following compound:
questions.solution:
The reactant molecule contains two aromatic rings. The right ring is fused to an electron-withdrawing lactam carbonyl group, making it deactivated. The left ring is attached to an amide nitrogen, a methoxy group ($-\text{OCH}_3$), and a methyl group ($-\text{CH}_3$). Because the left ring possesses these electron-donating and activating groups, it is significantly more electron-rich and will be the primary site for electrophilic aromatic substitution (nitration).

To determine the position of nitration on the left ring, we analyze the directing effects of its substituents. Let the carbon attached to the nitrogen be C1. The structure shows the methyl group at the para position (C4) and the methoxy group at a meta position (C3), which is adjacent to the methyl group.

The regioselectivity is dictated by the strongest activating group, which is the methoxy group at C3 due to its strong $+M$ resonance effect. As an ortho/para director, the $-\text{OCH}_3$ group directs the incoming nitronium ion ($\text{NO}_2^+$) to its ortho positions (C2 and C4) and its para position (C6).

Since position C4 is occupied by the methyl group, electrophilic attack must occur at C2 or C6. The amide nitrogen at C1 is also an ortho/para director, and it synergistically directs the electrophile to these same positions (C2 and C6).

Position C2 is situated between the bulky amide nitrogen substituent and the methoxy group, resulting in severe steric hindrance. Conversely, position C6 is flanked by the nitrogen substituent and a small hydrogen atom, making it much more sterically accessible.

Consequently, nitration preferentially occurs at the less hindered, highly activated C6 position, which is para to the methoxy group and ortho to the nitrogen. This specific regioisomer is shown in option (3).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Terminal alkynes react with 9-borabicyclo[3.3.1]nonane (9-BBN) followed by aqueous alkaline hydrogen peroxide to yield aldehydes. This regioselectivity is observed because:
options: Steric and electronic effects direct the boron atom to add to the terminal carbon.
The reaction proceeds via an anti-Markovnikov radical addition.
Acid-catalysed electrophilic addition of water follows Markovnikov's rule.
Acid-catalysed electrophilic addition of water follows anti-Markovnikov's rule.
questions.solution:
Hydroboration-oxidation of terminal alkynes using a bulky borane like 9-BBN followed by aqueous alkaline hydrogen peroxide yields aldehydes.

The addition of the B-H bond across the carbon-carbon triple bond is a concerted syn-addition. The regioselectivity is governed by both steric and electronic factors.

Sterically, the bulky 9-BBN group preferentially attacks the less hindered terminal carbon atom.

Electronically, the boron atom acts as an electrophile. In the transition state, a partial positive charge develops on the more substituted carbon atom, which is better stabilized by the electron-donating alkyl group.

Thus, both steric and electronic effects direct the boron atom to add to the terminal carbon, leading to anti-Markovnikov hydration and the formation of an aldehyde after tautomerization.

Answer: Steric and electronic effects direct the boron atom to add to the terminal carbon.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
For which of the following compounds does the addition of $\mathrm{HX}$ proceed with anti-Markovnikov regioselectivity?
(i) $\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{NO}_{2}$
(ii) $\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CHO}$
(iii) $\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CN}$
(iv) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$
options: (i), (ii) and (iv)
(i), (ii) and (iii)
(i) and (iii)
(i) and (ii)
questions.solution:
The addition of $\mathrm{HX}$ to alkenes generally follows Markovnikov's rule, where the electrophile ($\mathrm{H}^{+}$) adds to the carbon with more hydrogen atoms to form the most stable carbocation intermediate.

However, when a strong electron-withdrawing group (EWG) is attached directly to the double bond, the polarization of the $\pi$-bond is reversed due to the $-M$ and $-I$ effects. The resonance structures show that the terminal $\beta$-carbon acquires a partial positive charge, while the $\alpha$-carbon acquires a partial negative charge:
$\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{EWG} \leftrightarrow ^{+}\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{EWG}^{-}$

Because of this polarization, the electrophile $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ attacks the $\alpha$-carbon, and the nucleophile $\mathrm{X}^{-}$ attacks the $\beta$-carbon, resulting in anti-Markovnikov regioselectivity. Alternatively, if a carbocation were to form, placing it adjacent to a strong EWG would highly destabilize it, so the less destabilized primary carbocation forms instead.

In the given compounds:
(i) $-\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ is a strong EWG ($-M, -I$).
(ii) $-\mathrm{CHO}$ is a strong EWG ($-M, -I$).
(iii) $-\mathrm{CN}$ is a strong EWG ($-M, -I$).
Thus, compounds (i), (ii), and (iii) will undergo anti-Markovnikov addition of $\mathrm{HX}$.

(iv) In $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$, the $-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ group is electron-donating ($+I$, hyperconjugation). Protonation at the terminal carbon yields a stable secondary carbocation, leading to normal Markovnikov addition.

Therefore, compounds (i), (ii), and (iii) proceed with anti-Markovnikov regioselectivity.

Answer: (i), (ii) and (iii)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the alkene(s) that would yield the two products shown in the following ozonolysis reaction.


(i)
(ii)
(iii)
(iv)
options: Only (i)
(ii) and (iii)
Only (ii)
(iii) and (iv)
questions.solution:
The given ozonolysis reaction produces 2-oxohexanedial ($OHC-C(=O)-CH_2-CH_2-CH_2-CHO$) and formaldehyde ($H_2C=O$).

Let us analyze the ozonolysis products of each given alkene:

(i) 4-methylenecyclohexene: Ozonolysis cleaves the endocyclic and exocyclic double bonds to yield 3-oxohexanedial ($OHC-CH_2-C(=O)-CH_2-CH_2-CHO$) and formaldehyde. This does not match the given product.

(ii) 3-methylenecyclohexene: Ozonolysis cleaves the endocyclic and exocyclic double bonds to yield 2-oxohexanedial ($OHC-C(=O)-CH_2-CH_2-CH_2-CHO$) and formaldehyde. This matches the given products.

(iii) 1-vinylcyclopentene: Ozonolysis cleaves the ring double bond and the vinyl double bond to yield 2-oxohexanedial ($OHC-C(=O)-CH_2-CH_2-CH_2-CHO$) and formaldehyde. This matches the given products.

(iv) 3-vinylcyclopentene: Ozonolysis cleaves the two double bonds to yield 2-formylpentanedial ($OHC-CH_2-CH_2-CH(CHO)-CHO$) and formaldehyde. This does not match the given product.

Thus, alkenes (ii) and (iii) yield the correct products.

Answer: (ii) and (iii)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: When methyl benzoate is nitrated using a mixture of $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}$, the major product obtained is methyl $m$-nitrobenzoate. This is because:
options: The $-\mathrm{OCH}_{3}$ group is a meta director.
Nitration usually occurs at the meta position.
The $-\mathrm{CO}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ group is a meta director.
The $-\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ group is deactivating and a meta director.
questions.solution:
Methyl benzoate contains the $-\mathrm{CO}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ group attached directly to the benzene ring.

The $-\mathrm{CO}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ group is an electron-withdrawing group due to its $-I$ and $-R$ effects.

Electron-withdrawing groups deactivate the benzene ring towards electrophilic aromatic substitution and decrease the electron density at the ortho and para positions more than at the meta position.

As a result, the incoming electrophile ($\mathrm{NO}_{2}^{+}$) preferentially attacks the meta position, making the $-\mathrm{CO}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ group a meta director.

Thus, the nitration of methyl benzoate yields methyl $m$-nitrobenzoate as the major product.

Answer: The $-\mathrm{CO}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ group is a meta director.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product $[\mathrm{X}]$ for the following Friedel-Crafts alkylation reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reaction is a Friedel-Crafts alkylation where $\mathrm{CH_3Cl}$ and $\mathrm{AlCl_3}$ generate the methyl cation electrophile ($\mathrm{CH_3^+}$).

The reactant is a biphenyl derivative containing two substituted rings:
1. One ring has a $-\mathrm{NO_2}$ group, which is strongly electron-withdrawing and deactivates the ring towards electrophilic aromatic substitution.
2. The other ring has an $-\mathrm{OCH_3}$ group, which is strongly electron-donating (via +R effect) and activates the ring.

Because the ring with the $-\mathrm{OCH_3}$ group is much more electron-rich, the electrophilic attack will exclusively occur on this activated ring.

The $-\mathrm{OCH_3}$ group is an ortho/para director. Since the para position of this ring is already occupied by the 4'-nitrophenyl group, the incoming methyl electrophile will be directed to the position ortho to the $-\mathrm{OCH_3}$ group.

Therefore, the methyl group attaches to the carbon adjacent to the $-\mathrm{OCH_3}$ group, yielding 3-methyl-4-methoxy-4'-nitrobiphenyl as the major product. This matches the structure given in Option (A).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Consider the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The reactant is a substituted aniline derivative. Let's determine its exact structure from the given image.
The central benzene ring has four substituents:
1. An $-\text{NH}_2$ group.
2. Two $-\text{CH}_3$ groups.
3. A benzyl group ($-\text{CH}_2\text{C}_6\text{H}_5$).

By tracing the adjacencies on the ring in the reactant image:
- The $-\text{NH}_2$ group is adjacent to both $-\text{CH}_3$ groups. This means the $-\text{NH}_2$ group is at position 1, and the $-\text{CH}_3$ groups are at positions 2 and 6 (ortho positions).
- The benzyl group ($-\text{CH}_2\text{C}_6\text{H}_5$) is adjacent to one of the $-\text{CH}_3$ groups. This places it at position 3 (meta to $-\text{NH}_2$).
Thus, the reactant is 3-benzyl-2,6-dimethylaniline.

Now, we consider the electrophilic aromatic substitution (bromination with $\text{Br}_2/\text{CCl}_4$).
- The $-\text{NH}_2$ group is a strongly activating and ortho/para-directing group. Its directing effect will dominate over the weakly activating alkyl groups ($-\text{CH}_3$ and $-\text{CH}_2\text{Ph}$).
- The ortho positions (C2 and C6) with respect to the $-\text{NH}_2$ group are already occupied by the two $-\text{CH}_3$ groups.
- Therefore, the incoming electrophile ($\text{Br}^+$) will be directed to the para position (C4) with respect to the $-\text{NH}_2$ group.

The para position (C4) is unsubstituted in the reactant. Bromination will occur at this position to give 4-bromo-3-benzyl-2,6-dimethylaniline as the major product.

Looking at the given options:
- Option (A) shows bromination at the meta position (C5) relative to $-\text{NH}_2$.
- Option (C) shows bromination at the para position (C4) relative to $-\text{NH}_2$. The structure has the $-\text{NH}_2$ group at the top-right, $-\text{CH}_3$ groups at the top and bottom-right, the benzyl group at the bottom, and the Br atom at the bottom-left, which is exactly para to the $-\text{NH}_2$ group.

Therefore, the major product is given by Option (C).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the reagent 'X' that yields the given major product in the following reaction:
options: $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{Cl} \text{ / anhydrous } \mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$
All of the above
$\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}_{3} \text{ / } \mathrm{HF}$
questions.solution:
The given reaction is an electrophilic aromatic substitution where the major product is isopropylbenzene (cumene). This requires the generation of an isopropyl carbocation, $\mathrm{CH_3-\overset{+}{C}H-CH_3}$, as the electrophile.

Let us evaluate the given reagents:

For option (A): $\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2Cl}$ with anhydrous $\mathrm{AlCl_3}$ initially forms a primary carbocation ($\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2^+}$). This undergoes a 1,2-hydride shift to form the more stable secondary carbocation, $\mathrm{CH_3-\overset{+}{C}H-CH_3}$. Attack on benzene yields isopropylbenzene.

For option (C): $\mathrm{CH_2=CH-CH_3}$ reacts with $\mathrm{HF}$ to undergo protonation. Following Markovnikov's rule, the proton adds to the terminal carbon to generate the more stable secondary carbocation, $\mathrm{CH_3-\overset{+}{C}H-CH_3}$, which then reacts with benzene to form isopropylbenzene.

For option (D): $\mathrm{CH_3-CH(Cl)-CH_3}$ with anhydrous $\mathrm{AlCl_3}$ directly forms the secondary carbocation, $\mathrm{CH_3-\overset{+}{C}H-CH_3}$, leading to the formation of isopropylbenzene.

Since all the given reagents generate the same isopropyl carbocation as the electrophile, they all yield isopropylbenzene as the major product.

Answer: All of the above

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reactant is phenyl benzoate, which contains two benzene rings. One ring is attached to the carbonyl carbon (benzoyl ring) and the other is attached to the oxygen atom (phenyl ring).

The $-O-CO-C_6H_5$ group attached to the phenyl ring has an oxygen atom with lone pairs, which donates electron density to the ring via the $+R$ effect. This activates the phenyl ring towards electrophilic aromatic substitution and makes it ortho/para directing.

On the other hand, the $-CO-O-C_6H_5$ group attached to the benzoyl ring is electron-withdrawing due to the $-R$ effect of the carbonyl group, which deactivates the ring and makes it meta directing.

Since electrophilic aromatic substitution occurs preferentially on the more activated ring, nitration will take place on the phenyl ring attached to the oxygen atom. Due to the steric hindrance caused by the bulky benzoate group at the ortho positions, the major product will be the para-substituted isomer.

Thus, the major product is 4-nitrophenyl benzoate, where the $-NO_2$ group is at the para position of the phenyl ring.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The major product obtained upon further nitration of $p$-nitrotoluene is:
questions.solution:
In $p$-nitrotoluene, the methyl group ($-CH_3$) is an activating group due to $+I$ and hyperconjugation effects, directing the incoming electrophile to the ortho and para positions. Since the para position is already occupied, it directs the electrophile to the ortho position.

The nitro group ($-NO_2$) is a strongly deactivating group due to $-I$ and $-M$ effects, directing the incoming electrophile to the meta position.

The positions ortho to the $-CH_3$ group are identical to the positions meta to the $-NO_2$ group. Thus, both substituents reinforce each other's directing effects towards the same carbon atoms on the benzene ring.

Therefore, during further nitration, the incoming nitronium ion ($NO_2^+$) attacks the position ortho to the $-CH_3$ group, yielding 2,4-dinitrotoluene as the major product.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The reaction of isobutylene with an acid ($H^\oplus$) generates a stable tert-butyl carbocation, which acts as an electrophile:

$(\text{CH}_3)_2\text{C}=\text{CH}_2 + H^\oplus \rightarrow (\text{CH}_3)_3\text{C}^\oplus$

This electrophile will undergo an electrophilic aromatic substitution (Friedel-Crafts alkylation) with the given aromatic ring.

The reactant, $p$-methylanisole, has two substituents on the benzene ring: a methoxy group ($-\text{OCH}_3$) and a methyl group ($-\text{CH}_3$).

Both groups are activating and ortho/para directing. However, the $-\text{OCH}_3$ group is a much stronger activating group than the $-\text{CH}_3$ group due to the strong $+R$ (resonance) effect of the lone pairs on the oxygen atom, compared to the weaker $+H$ (hyperconjugation) and $+I$ (inductive) effects of the methyl group.

Therefore, the $-\text{OCH}_3$ group will dictate the regioselectivity of the incoming electrophile. Since the position para to the $-\text{OCH}_3$ group is already blocked by the $-\text{CH}_3$ group, the electrophile will attack at the position ortho to the $-\text{OCH}_3$ group.

Thus, the tert-butyl group attaches to the ring at the position ortho to the methoxy group, yielding the major product shown in Option (D).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds yields benzoic acid upon oxidation with an acidified $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$ solution?
options: All of these
questions.solution:
Oxidation of alkylbenzenes with acidified $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$ yields benzoic acid, provided there is at least one benzylic hydrogen atom present.

1. Toluene (methylbenzene) has three benzylic hydrogens. Upon oxidation with acidified $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$, the methyl group is oxidized to a carboxyl group, yielding benzoic acid.

2. Isopropylbenzene (cumene) has one benzylic hydrogen. The entire isopropyl group is oxidized to a carboxyl group by acidified $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$, also yielding benzoic acid.

3. 1-phenylpropyne is an internal alkyne. Vigorous oxidation of alkynes with acidified $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$ results in the cleavage of the triple bond, producing carboxylic acids. The cleavage of $Ph-C \equiv C-CH_3$ yields benzoic acid ($PhCOOH$) and acetic acid ($CH_3COOH$).

Since all three compounds yield benzoic acid upon oxidation with acidified $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$, the correct choice is 'All of these'.

Answer: All of these

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the product formed in the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reaction is the nitration of quinoline using a mixture of concentrated $\text{HNO}_3$ and $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$.

In quinoline, the pyridine ring is deactivated towards electrophilic aromatic substitution due to the electron-withdrawing nature of the nitrogen atom. This deactivation is further enhanced in the acidic medium due to the protonation of nitrogen to form a quinolinium ion.

Consequently, electrophilic substitution takes place preferentially on the more electron-rich benzene ring.

Attack of the electrophile ($\text{NO}_2^+$) at positions 5 and 8 produces intermediate arenium ions that are more stable than those formed by attack at positions 6 and 7. This is because the positive charge can be delocalized in such a way that the aromatic sextet of the pyridine ring is preserved in more resonance structures.

Thus, the nitration of quinoline yields a mixture of 5-nitroquinoline and 8-nitroquinoline as the major products.

Among the given options, the structure in option (C) corresponds to 5-nitroquinoline, which is one of the major mononitrated products.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction sequence:
questions.solution:
Step 1: Bromination
Benzene reacts with $Br_2$ in the presence of Lewis acid $FeBr_3$ to undergo electrophilic aromatic substitution, forming bromobenzene.

Step 2: Sulfonation
Bromobenzene reacts with $SO_3/H_2SO_4$ (fuming sulfuric acid). Bromine is an ortho/para-directing group. Due to steric hindrance at the ortho position, the major product is 4-bromobenzenesulfonic acid. The $-SO_3H$ group effectively blocks the para position.

Step 3: Friedel-Crafts Alkylation
Isobutyl bromide reacts with $AlCl_3$ to generate a primary isobutyl cation ($CH_3-CH(CH_3)-CH_2^+$). This carbocation undergoes a 1,2-hydride shift to form the more stable tertiary carbocation, the tert-butyl cation ($(CH_3)_3C^+$).
The tert-butyl cation acts as the electrophile. The $-Br$ group is ortho/para-directing (with para blocked), and the $-SO_3H$ group is meta-directing. Both groups direct the incoming electrophile to the position ortho to the bromine atom. This yields 4-bromo-3-tert-butylbenzenesulfonic acid.

Step 4: Desulfonation
Heating the intermediate with dilute acid ($H^\oplus/H_2O$) leads to desulfonation, removing the $-SO_3H$ blocking group. The final major product is 1-bromo-2-tert-butylbenzene.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the effective electrophiles involved in the sulphonation, acetylation, and formylation of benzene, respectively.
options: $\mathrm{SO}_{3}, \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CHO}, \mathrm{CO}+\mathrm{HCl}$
$\mathrm{SO}_{3}, \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{C} \equiv \stackrel{\oplus}{\mathrm{O}}, \stackrel{\oplus}{\mathrm{HC}} \mathrm{O}$
$\mathrm{SO}_{3}^{\oplus}, \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{C} \equiv \stackrel{\oplus}{\mathrm{O}}, \mathrm{H} \stackrel{\oplus}{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{O}$
$\mathrm{HSO}_{3}, \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CO}, \mathrm{HCO}$
questions.solution:
In the sulphonation of benzene, the active electrophile is the neutral sulfur trioxide molecule, $\mathrm{SO}_{3}$.

In the Friedel-Crafts acetylation of benzene, acetyl chloride reacts with a Lewis acid like $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$ to generate the acylium ion, $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{C}\equiv\stackrel{\oplus}{\mathrm{O}}$, which acts as the electrophile.

In the formylation of benzene (Gattermann-Koch reaction), carbon monoxide and hydrogen chloride react in the presence of anhydrous $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$ to produce the formyl cation, $\stackrel{\oplus}{\mathrm{HC}}\mathrm{O}$, which serves as the electrophile.

Answer: $\mathrm{SO}_{3}, \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{C} \equiv \stackrel{\oplus}{\mathrm{O}}, \stackrel{\oplus}{\mathrm{HC}} \mathrm{O}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reaction employs Zn(Hg) and concentrated HCl with heating, which are the standard reagents for the Clemmensen reduction.

The Clemmensen reduction specifically reduces carbonyl groups (aldehydes and ketones) to their corresponding alkane (methylene) groups. Therefore, the ketone group ($-\text{CO}-\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_3$) in the reactant will be reduced to a propyl group ($-\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_3$).

Additionally, the reactant contains a secondary benzylic alcohol group ($-\text{CH}(\text{OH})\text{CH}_3$). The reaction conditions are strongly acidic due to the presence of concentrated HCl. Under these conditions, benzylic alcohols readily undergo nucleophilic substitution ($S_N1$ mechanism). The hydroxyl group is protonated to form a good leaving group ($-\text{OH}_2^+$), which leaves to generate a resonance-stabilized benzylic carbocation. The chloride ion ($Cl^-$) from HCl then attacks this carbocation, resulting in the substitution of the $-\text{OH}$ group by a $-\text{Cl}$ atom to form a benzylic chloride ($-\text{CH}(\text{Cl})\text{CH}_3$).

Combining these two transformations, the major product will have the ketone reduced to an alkane and the alcohol substituted by a chlorine atom. This corresponds to the structure given in Option (A).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds is most reactive towards electrophilic aromatic nitration?
questions.solution:
The reactivity of an aromatic compound towards electrophilic aromatic substitution (such as nitration) depends on the electron density of the benzene ring. Electron-donating groups (activating groups) increase this electron density, making the ring more reactive, while electron-withdrawing groups (deactivating groups) decrease it.

Let us analyze the substituents in the given options:
1. In compound (A), the $-\text{OMe}$ group is a strongly activating group due to its strong $+M$ (mesomeric) effect, which significantly increases the electron density on the ring.
2. In compound (B), the $-\text{Me}$ group is a weakly activating group due to its $+I$ (inductive) effect and hyperconjugation.
3. In compound (C), the $-\text{COMe}$ group is a deactivating group due to its $-M$ and $-I$ effects.
4. In compound (D), the $-\text{COOMe}$ group is also a deactivating group due to its $-M$ and $-I$ effects.

Comparing the activating groups, $-\text{OMe}$ is a much stronger electron donor than $-\text{Me}$. The presence of deuterium (D) instead of hydrogen (H) on the benzene ring in compound (A) does not significantly affect the rate of nitration because the breaking of the $\text{C-H}$ or $\text{C-D}$ bond is not the rate-determining step (absence of primary kinetic isotope effect).

Therefore, the compound with the $-\text{OMe}$ group, pentadeuteroanisole (A), has the highest electron density on the aromatic ring and is the most reactive towards electrophilic aromatic nitration.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reaction is the electrophilic aromatic chlorination of (trifluoromethyl)benzene.

The reagents $\text{Cl}_2$ and $\text{FeCl}_3$ generate the electrophile $\text{Cl}^+$.

The $-\text{CF}_3$ group is strongly electron-withdrawing due to the strong -I effect of the highly electronegative fluorine atoms.

As a result, the $-\text{CF}_3$ group is ring-deactivating and acts as a meta-directing group.

Therefore, the incoming electrophile ($\text{Cl}^+$) will attack the meta position of the benzene ring.

The major product formed is 1-chloro-3-(trifluoromethyl)benzene, which corresponds to the structure in option (C).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What is the expected major product when an excess of toluene reacts with dichloromethane ($\mathrm{CH}_2\mathrm{Cl}_2$) in the presence of anhydrous $\mathrm{AlCl}_3$?
questions.solution:
The reaction of toluene with dichloromethane ($\mathrm{CH}_2\mathrm{Cl}_2$) in the presence of anhydrous $\mathrm{AlCl}_3$ is an example of Friedel-Crafts alkylation.

First, $\mathrm{CH}_2\mathrm{Cl}_2$ reacts with the Lewis acid $\mathrm{AlCl}_3$ to generate an electrophile, $\mathrm{CH}_2\mathrm{Cl}^+$.

Toluene undergoes electrophilic aromatic substitution with this electrophile. The $-\mathrm{CH}_3$ group on toluene is an activating and ortho/para-directing group. Due to steric hindrance at the ortho position, the para product is favored, yielding 4-methylbenzyl chloride as an intermediate.

Since toluene is present in excess, the intermediate 4-methylbenzyl chloride can undergo a second Friedel-Crafts alkylation. It reacts with $\mathrm{AlCl}_3$ to form a resonance-stabilized benzylic carbocation, $\mathrm{p}-\mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{C}_6\mathrm{H}_4\mathrm{CH}_2^+$.

This carbocation then attacks another molecule of toluene, again predominantly at the sterically less hindered para position.

The final major product is di(p-tolyl)methane, which has a $-\mathrm{CH}_2-$ group bridging two para-substituted toluene rings.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the following two-step reaction sequence:
questions.solution:
The reaction sequence involves two steps: electrophilic aromatic substitution followed by oxidation.

Step 1: Bromination
The reactant is a biphenyl derivative with two different rings. The left ring contains a strongly deactivating nitro group ($-NO_2$), which withdraws electron density via $-I$ and $-M$ effects. The right ring contains a methyl group ($-CH_3$), which is an activating group due to its $+I$ and hyperconjugation effects. Because the right ring is more electron-rich, electrophilic aromatic substitution (bromination with $Br_2/FeCl_3$) will occur on this ring.

On the right ring, the $-CH_3$ group is ortho/para directing. Since the para position is already occupied by the 3-nitrophenyl group, the incoming electrophile ($Br^+$) will be directed to the ortho position relative to the $-CH_3$ group. This forms the intermediate 2-bromo-4-(3-nitrophenyl)toluene.

Step 2: Oxidation
The intermediate is then treated with a strong oxidizing agent, $K_2Cr_2O_7$ in acidic medium ($H_2SO_4$) with heating. These conditions specifically oxidize alkyl side chains attached to an aromatic ring into carboxylic acid groups ($-COOH$). The $-CH_3$ group is oxidized to a $-COOH$ group, while the bromine atom and the nitro group remain unaffected.

The final major product is 2-bromo-4-(3-nitrophenyl)benzoic acid, where the bromine atom is ortho to the newly formed carboxylic acid group. This structure corresponds to Option (B).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the appropriate reagents A and B for the following transformation:
options: Reagent A: $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COCl} / \mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$; Reagent B: $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{NNH}_{2}, \mathrm{KOH}, \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$, heat
Reagent A: $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COCl} / \mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$; Reagent B: $\mathrm{Sn}, \mathrm{HCl}$, heat
Reagent A: $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{Cl} / \mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$; Reagent B: $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$, heat
Reagent A: $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}, \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}$; Reagent B: $\mathrm{Sn}, \mathrm{HCl}$, heat
questions.solution:
The given transformation is the conversion of benzene to benzoic acid.

Evaluating the given options:

For option (A): Benzene reacts with $\text{CH}_3\text{COCl} / \text{AlCl}_3$ to give acetophenone, which on Wolff-Kishner reduction ($\text{H}_2\text{NNH}_2, \text{KOH}$, heat) gives ethylbenzene.

For option (B): Benzene reacts with $\text{CH}_3\text{COCl} / \text{AlCl}_3$ to give acetophenone. $\text{Sn}/\text{HCl}$ does not convert it to benzoic acid.

For option (C): Benzene undergoes Friedel-Crafts alkylation with $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{Cl} / \text{AlCl}_3$ to form ethylbenzene. Ethylbenzene, having benzylic hydrogens, undergoes oxidation with hot $\text{KMnO}_4$ to form benzoic acid. This matches the required transformation.

For option (D): Benzene undergoes nitration with $\text{HNO}_3, \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ to form nitrobenzene, which on reduction with $\text{Sn}, \text{HCl}$ gives aniline.

Thus, Reagent A is $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{Cl} / \text{AlCl}_3$ and Reagent B is $\text{KMnO}_4$, heat.

Answer: Reagent A: $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{Cl} / \mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$; Reagent B: $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$, heat

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct decreasing order of reactivity towards electrophilic aromatic substitution for the following compounds:

I.
II.
III.
IV.
options: III > II > IV > I
IV > III > II > I
I > II > III > IV
II > I > IV > III
questions.solution:
The reactivity of aromatic compounds towards electrophilic aromatic substitution depends on the electron density of the benzene ring. Electron-donating groups (EDG) increase the electron density and activate the ring, making it more reactive. Electron-withdrawing groups (EWG) decrease the electron density and deactivate the ring, making it less reactive.

Let us analyze the substituents in the given compounds:
I. Benzoic acid has a $-\text{COOH}$ group, which is strongly electron-withdrawing due to its $-M$ and $-I$ effects. It strongly deactivates the ring.
II. Toluene has a $-\text{CH}_3$ group, which is electron-donating due to hyperconjugation ($+H$) and $+I$ effects. It activates the ring.
III. Anisole has an $-\text{OCH}_3$ group, which is strongly electron-donating due to the $+M$ effect of the lone pairs on oxygen (which outweighs its $-I$ effect). It strongly activates the ring.
IV. Benzene has no substituents and serves as the reference.

Comparing the activating power of the groups, the $+M$ effect of $-\text{OCH}_3$ is stronger than the hyperconjugation of $-\text{CH}_3$. Therefore, anisole (III) is the most reactive, followed by toluene (II). Benzene (IV) is next, and benzoic acid (I) is the least reactive due to the deactivating $-\text{COOH}$ group.

The correct decreasing order of reactivity is III > II > IV > I.

Answer: III > II > IV > I

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product (B) in the following reaction sequence:
questions.solution:
The first step involves the Friedel-Crafts alkylation of benzene. Since benzene is present in excess, both chloromethyl groups of $p$-bis(chloromethyl)benzene react with benzene in the presence of anhydrous $AlCl_3$ to form $p$-dibenzylbenzene as product (A).

$Cl-CH_2-C_6H_4-CH_2-Cl + 2C_6H_6 \xrightarrow{AlCl_3} C_6H_5-CH_2-C_6H_4-CH_2-C_6H_5$

In the second step, product (A) is treated with N-bromosuccinimide (NBS) in the presence of light ($h\nu$). NBS is a specific reagent for free-radical bromination at allylic and benzylic positions.

The $CH_2$ groups in product (A) are benzylic to two benzene rings, making the corresponding free radical highly stabilized by resonance. Thus, bromination occurs at one of these benzylic positions to give product (B).

$C_6H_5-CH_2-C_6H_4-CH_2-C_6H_5 \xrightarrow{NBS/h\nu} C_6H_5-CH_2-C_6H_4-CH(Br)-C_6H_5$

This structure corresponds to the one given in Option (C).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following was NOT historically proposed as a structural formula for benzene?
options: None of these
questions.solution:
Historically, several structures were proposed for benzene before its actual resonance structure was established:

1. The structure in option (A) is Claus's benzene, proposed by Adolf Claus in 1867, which features diagonal bonds connecting opposite carbon atoms in a hexagon.
2. The structure in option (C) is Ladenburg's benzene (prismane), proposed by Albert Ladenburg in 1869.
3. The structure in option (D) is Dewar's benzene, proposed by James Dewar in 1867.

Since all the given structures were historically proposed as structural formulas for benzene, the correct choice is "None of these".

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following compounds in the decreasing order of their reactivity towards electrophilic aromatic substitution:
(I) $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{CH}_{3}$
(II) $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{6}$
(III) $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{Br}$
(IV) $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\stackrel{\oplus}{\mathrm{N}}\mathrm{R}_{3}$
(V) $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{NMe}_{2}$
questions.solution:
The reactivity of aromatic compounds towards electrophilic aromatic substitution depends on the electron density of the benzene ring, which is determined by the nature of the substituent attached to it.

Electron-donating groups increase the electron density and activate the ring, making it more reactive than benzene. Electron-withdrawing groups decrease the electron density and deactivate the ring, making it less reactive than benzene.

Analyzing the given compounds:
(V) $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{NMe}_{2}$: The $-\mathrm{NMe}_{2}$ group is strongly activating due to its strong $+M$ effect. This makes it the most reactive.
(I) $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{CH}_{3}$: The $-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ group is activating due to its $+I$ effect and hyperconjugation. It is more reactive than benzene.
(II) $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{6}$: Benzene serves as the reference standard.
(III) $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{Br}$: The $-\mathrm{Br}$ group is deactivating because its $-I$ effect outweighs its $+M$ effect. It is less reactive than benzene.
(IV) $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\stackrel{\oplus}{\mathrm{N}}\mathrm{R}_{3}$: The $-\stackrel{\oplus}{\mathrm{N}}\mathrm{R}_{3}$ group is strongly deactivating due to its very strong $-I$ effect and the absence of any lone pair for $+M$ effect. It is the least reactive.

Therefore, the decreasing order of reactivity is (V) > (I) > (II) > (III) > (IV).

Answer: (V) > (I) > (II) > (III) > (IV)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reaction is the halogenation of benzene, which is an example of electrophilic aromatic substitution.

In the presence of a Lewis acid catalyst like $\text{FeCl}_3$, chlorine ($\text{Cl}_2$) generates the electrophile, chloronium ion ($\text{Cl}^+$).

$\text{Cl}_2 + \text{FeCl}_3 \rightarrow \text{Cl}^+ + \text{FeCl}_4^-$

The electrophile $\text{Cl}^+$ attacks the electron-rich benzene ring to form a sigma complex, which then loses a proton to yield chlorobenzene.

Since only one equivalent of $\text{Cl}_2$ is used, the reaction stops at mono-substitution.

Therefore, the major product formed is chlorobenzene.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The reaction involves the Friedel-Crafts alkylation of acetanilide with oxetane in the presence of a Lewis acid ($BF_3$) and water.

1. Generation of the electrophile: The Lewis acid $BF_3$ coordinates with the oxygen atom of the oxetane ring, polarizing the $C-O$ bond and making the adjacent carbon atoms highly electrophilic. This forms the complex:
$\text{Oxetane} + BF_3 \rightarrow \text{Oxetane}-O^{+}-BF_3^{-}$

2. Electrophilic aromatic substitution: Acetanilide has an $-NHCOCH_3$ group, which is an activating and ortho/para-directing group due to the +R effect of the nitrogen lone pair. Because of the steric bulk of the $-NHCOCH_3$ group, the incoming electrophile will preferentially attack at the less hindered para position.

3. Ring opening and hydrolysis: The $\pi$-electrons of the benzene ring attack the electrophilic carbon of the oxetane complex, leading to the opening of the four-membered ring. This forms a 3-carbon alkyl chain with an oxygen atom at the end.

4. Aqueous workup: The addition of $H_2O$ hydrolyzes the $-O-BF_3^{-}$ intermediate to a hydroxyl ($-OH$) group.

The resulting major product is a para-substituted aromatic compound with a 3-hydroxypropyl chain, which is $N$-(4-(3-hydroxypropyl)phenyl)acetamide. This matches the structure given in Option (C).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reaction involves the treatment of a ketone (1-phenylbutan-1-one) with zinc amalgam $\text{Zn(Hg)}$ and concentrated hydrochloric acid $\text{HCl}$ under heating.

This is a standard Clemmensen reduction. In this reaction, the carbonyl group ($>\text{C=O}$) of aldehydes and ketones is reduced to a methylene group ($>\text{CH}_2$).

The reactant is $Ph-\text{CO}-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_3$.

Upon Clemmensen reduction, the carbonyl carbon is reduced to a $-\text{CH}_2-$ group, yielding butylbenzene ($Ph-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_3$) as the major product.

Comparing this with the given options, the structure in option (C) corresponds to butylbenzene.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product for the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reaction is a Friedel-Crafts alkylation of benzene.

First, 1-chlorobutane reacts with the Lewis acid catalyst $AlCl_3$ to generate a primary carbocation.

$CH_3CH_2CH_2CH_2Cl + AlCl_3 \rightarrow CH_3CH_2CH_2CH_2^+ + AlCl_4^-$

The primary carbocation is relatively unstable and undergoes a 1,2-hydride shift to form a more stable secondary carbocation.

$CH_3CH_2CH_2CH_2^+ \rightarrow CH_3CH_2CH^+CH_3$

The secondary carbocation acts as an electrophile and attacks the benzene ring, leading to the formation of sec-butylbenzene as the major product.

The structure of sec-butylbenzene corresponds to the molecule shown in option (B).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds is/are more reactive than benzene towards electrophilic nitration?
options: All of these
questions.solution:
Electrophilic nitration involves the attack of an electrophile ($NO_2^+$) on the aromatic ring. The reactivity of an aromatic compound towards electrophilic substitution depends on the electron density of the ring and the stability of the intermediate arenium ion.

1. Toluene: The $-CH_3$ group is an electron-donating group due to hyperconjugation and the +I effect. It increases the electron density on the benzene ring and stabilizes the intermediate carbocation, making toluene more reactive than benzene.

2. Naphthalene: The intermediate arenium ion formed during the electrophilic attack on naphthalene is highly stabilized by resonance. The positive charge is delocalized over a more extensive $\pi$ system while retaining an intact benzene ring, which preserves a significant amount of resonance energy. Thus, naphthalene is much more reactive than benzene.

3. Biphenyl: One phenyl ring acts as an electron-donating group to the other via resonance (+R effect). The intermediate carbocation formed upon electrophilic attack is stabilized by the delocalization of the positive charge into the adjacent phenyl ring. Therefore, biphenyl is also more reactive than benzene.

Since all three compounds stabilize the intermediate arenium ion better than benzene, they are all more reactive towards electrophilic nitration.

Answer: All of these

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reaction is an electrophilic aromatic substitution, specifically halogenation.

Benzene reacts with chlorine in the presence of a Lewis acid catalyst such as $\text{FeCl}_3$.

The catalyst $\text{FeCl}_3$ polarizes the $\text{Cl}-\text{Cl}$ bond to generate the electrophile $\text{Cl}^+$.

As $1$ equivalent of $\text{Cl}_2$ is provided, monosubstitution takes place on the benzene ring.

The resulting major product is chlorobenzene.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reaction is a Friedel-Crafts alkylation.

Step 1: The alkyl halide, 1-chlorobutane, reacts with the Lewis acid catalyst $AlCl_3$ to generate a primary carbocation.
$CH_3-CH_2-CH_2-CH_2-Cl + AlCl_3 \rightarrow CH_3-CH_2-CH_2-CH_2^+ + AlCl_4^-$

Step 2: The primary carbocation ($1^{\circ}$) is relatively unstable and undergoes a 1,2-hydride shift to form a more stable secondary carbocation ($2^{\circ}$).
$CH_3-CH_2-CH_2-CH_2^+ \xrightarrow{1,2-H^- \text{ shift}} CH_3-CH_2-CH^+-CH_3$

Step 3: The rearranged secondary carbocation acts as an electrophile and attacks the benzene ring, leading to electrophilic aromatic substitution.
$C_6H_6 + CH_3-CH_2-CH^+-CH_3 \rightarrow C_6H_5-CH(CH_3)(CH_2CH_3) + H^+$

The major product formed is sec-butylbenzene (2-phenylbutane), which corresponds to option (A).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following reaction sequences is most suitable for the synthesis of 1-bromo-3-propylbenzene?
questions.solution:
The target molecule is 1-bromo-3-propylbenzene, which has a bromine atom and a propyl group in a meta relationship on the benzene ring.

Since both the bromo ($-\text{Br}$) and propyl ($-\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_3$) groups are ortho/para-directing, we cannot introduce them directly one after the other to get a meta product. We must use a meta-directing precursor for one of the groups.

The propyl group can be synthesized by the Friedel-Crafts acylation of benzene to form a ketone, followed by its reduction. The acyl group ($-\text{CO}-\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_3$) is strongly electron-withdrawing and meta-directing.

Step 1: Friedel-Crafts acylation of benzene with propanoyl chloride ($\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{COCl}$) and $\text{AlCl}_3$ yields propiophenone.

Step 2: Bromination of propiophenone with $\text{Br}_2$ and $\text{FeBr}_3$ directs the incoming bromine to the meta position, yielding m-bromopropiophenone.

Step 3: Clemmensen reduction using $\text{Zn(Hg)}$ and $\text{HCl}$ reduces the carbonyl group to a methylene group, converting m-bromopropiophenone to 1-bromo-3-propylbenzene.

This sequence corresponds to Option (A).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify reagents A and B for the following reaction sequence:
options: Reagent A: $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COCl} / \mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$; Reagent B: $\mathrm{Na}_{2}\mathrm{Cr}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{7}$, heat
Reagent A: $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}, \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}$; Reagent B: $\mathrm{RCO}_{3}\mathrm{H}$, heat
Reagent A: $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COCl} / \mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$; Reagent B: $\mathrm{H}_{2}, \mathrm{Ni}$, heat
Reagent A: $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{Cl} / \mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$; Reagent B: $\mathrm{Na}_{2}\mathrm{Cr}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{7}$, heat
questions.solution:
The given reaction sequence converts benzene to benzoic acid.

First, benzene undergoes Friedel-Crafts alkylation with ethyl chloride ($\mathrm{CH_3CH_2Cl}$) in the presence of a Lewis acid catalyst ($\mathrm{AlCl_3}$) to form ethylbenzene.

$\mathrm{C_6H_6} + \mathrm{CH_3CH_2Cl} \rightarrow \mathrm{C_6H_5CH_2CH_3} + \mathrm{HCl}$

Next, ethylbenzene is subjected to vigorous oxidation. Since it has benzylic hydrogens, the entire alkyl side chain is oxidized to a carboxylic acid group upon treatment with a strong oxidizing agent like sodium dichromate ($\mathrm{Na_2Cr_2O_7}$) under heating.

$\mathrm{C_6H_5CH_2CH_3} \rightarrow \mathrm{C_6H_5COOH}$

If Reagent A were $\mathrm{CH_3COCl} / \mathrm{AlCl_3}$, the intermediate would be acetophenone. The standard method to convert acetophenone to benzoic acid is the haloform reaction, making the alkylation-oxidation route the most appropriate standard sequence.
