Answer: Reagent A: $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{Cl} / \mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$; Reagent B: $\mathrm{Na}_{2}\mathrm{Cr}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{7}$, heat

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the following Friedel-Crafts reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reaction is a Friedel-Crafts acylation. The electrophile is the acylium ion, $CH_3C^+=O$, generated from acetyl chloride and $AlCl_3$.

The substrate is 3-methoxy-4'-nitrobiphenyl. It has two aromatic rings:
1. The left ring contains a methoxy group ($-OCH_3$), which is a strongly activating and ortho/para-directing group due to its $+M$ effect.
2. The right ring contains a nitro group ($-NO_2$), which is a strongly deactivating group.

Electrophilic aromatic substitution will preferentially occur on the more electron-rich left ring.

Let's number the left ring starting from the carbon attached to the right ring as C1. The $-OCH_3$ group is at C3.
The $-OCH_3$ group directs the incoming electrophile to its ortho (C2, C4) and para (C6) positions.

Let's evaluate these positions:
- Position C2: It is located between the $-OCH_3$ group and the bulky $p$-nitrophenyl group. This position is highly sterically hindered, making substitution here very unfavorable.
- Position C6: It is para to the $-OCH_3$ group but ortho to the bulky $p$-nitrophenyl group. The $p$-nitrophenyl group exerts a strong electron-withdrawing inductive effect ($-I$), which is most pronounced at the closer ortho position (C6). Additionally, the steric hindrance from the adjacent aryl ring makes this position less favorable.
- Position C4: It is ortho to the $-OCH_3$ group and para to the $p$-nitrophenyl group. This position is much less sterically hindered because it is far from the bulky aryl group. Furthermore, being para to the aryl group, it experiences a weaker deactivating $-I$ effect compared to C6.

Due to both steric and electronic (inductive) factors, the electrophilic attack is most favored at position C4.

Looking at the given options:
- In Option (A), the acetyl group is at C4 (ortho to $-OCH_3$ and para to the aryl group).
- In Option (B), the acetyl group is at C2.
- In Option (C), the acetyl group is at C6.
- In Option (D), the acetyl group is at C5 (meta to $-OCH_3$).

Therefore, the major product is the one shown in Option (A).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the following reaction sequence.
options: None of these
questions.solution:
Chlorination of toluene with $Cl_2$ in the presence of $AlCl_3$ occurs via electrophilic aromatic substitution. The $-CH_3$ group is ortho/para directing. Due to steric hindrance at the ortho position, the major product is p-chlorotoluene.

Nitration of p-chlorotoluene with $HNO_3$ and $H_2SO_4$ introduces a nitro group. The activating $-CH_3$ group dominates over the deactivating $-Cl$ group in directing the incoming electrophile. Thus, nitration occurs ortho to the methyl group, yielding 2-nitro-4-chlorotoluene.

Catalytic hydrogenation with $H_2$ and $Ni$ selectively reduces the $-NO_2$ group to an $-NH_2$ group, forming 2-amino-4-chlorotoluene.

Diazotization of the amino group with $NaNO_2$ and $H_3O^+$ followed by heating with $BF_4^-$ (Balz-Schiemann reaction) replaces the diazonium group with a fluorine atom, producing 2-fluoro-4-chlorotoluene.

This final product corresponds to the structure in option (A).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The reaction of benzene with $Cl_2$ in the presence of a Lewis acid catalyst such as $FeCl_3$ is an electrophilic aromatic substitution.

The Lewis acid $FeCl_3$ reacts with $Cl_2$ to generate the chloronium ion electrophile ($Cl^+$).

The electrophile $Cl^+$ attacks the electron-rich benzene ring, replacing one hydrogen atom to form chlorobenzene.

The overall reaction is:
$C_6H_6 + Cl_2 \xrightarrow{FeCl_3} C_6H_5Cl + HCl$

Since one equivalent of $Cl_2$ is used, mono-chlorination takes place, resulting in chlorobenzene as the major product.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify a possible product for the following reaction sequence:


(i) $\mathrm{Cl}_{2} / \mathrm{FeCl}_{3}$
(ii) $\mathrm{Na}_{2}\mathrm{Cr}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{7}, \mathrm{H}^{+}$, heat
(iii) $\mathrm{PCl}_{3}$
(iv) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{OH}$
questions.solution:
The given starting material is toluene.

Step (i): Reaction with $\mathrm{Cl}_{2} / \mathrm{FeCl}_{3}$ is an electrophilic aromatic substitution. The $-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ group is an activating and ortho/para-directing group. This yields a mixture of o-chlorotoluene and p-chlorotoluene.

Step (ii): Reaction with $\mathrm{Na}_{2}\mathrm{Cr}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{7}, \mathrm{H}^{+}$ and heat causes the oxidation of the benzylic $-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ group to a carboxylic acid ($-\mathrm{COOH}$) group. This produces o-chlorobenzoic acid and p-chlorobenzoic acid.

Step (iii): Treatment with $\mathrm{PCl}_{3}$ converts the carboxylic acid group into an acyl chloride ($-\mathrm{COCl}$) group, forming o-chlorobenzoyl chloride and p-chlorobenzoyl chloride.

Step (iv): Reaction with methanol ($\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{OH}$) is an alcoholysis reaction that converts the acyl chloride into a methyl ester ($-\mathrm{COOCH}_{3}$). The final products are methyl o-chlorobenzoate and methyl p-chlorobenzoate.

Among the given options, methyl o-chlorobenzoate is present as option (D).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction sequence:
questions.solution:
The given reaction sequence consists of two main steps:

Step 1: Friedel-Crafts Acylation
Anisole (methoxybenzene) reacts with propanoyl chloride ($\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{COCl}$) in the presence of a Lewis acid catalyst, $\text{AlCl}_3$. The methoxy group ($-\text{OCH}_3$) is an electron-donating group, which activates the benzene ring and directs the incoming acyl electrophile to the ortho and para positions. Due to steric hindrance at the ortho position, the para product, 1-(4-methoxyphenyl)propan-1-one, is formed as the major product.

Step 2: Reduction
The intermediate ketone, 1-(4-methoxyphenyl)propan-1-one, is then treated with lithium aluminium hydride ($\text{LiAlH}_4$) in THF, followed by acid hydrolysis ($\text{H}_3\text{O}^+$). $\text{LiAlH}_4$ is a strong reducing agent that reduces the ketone group ($-\text{C=O}$) to a secondary alcohol ($-\text{CH(OH)}-$). The ether linkage ($-\text{OCH}_3$) remains unaffected by $\text{LiAlH}_4$.

Therefore, the final major product is 1-(4-methoxyphenyl)propan-1-ol, which corresponds to the structure in Option (B).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the major organic product $\mathrm{P}$ (after aqueous workup) in the following reaction:
$\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{C}\equiv\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{CH}_{3} \xrightarrow[\text{heat}]{\mathrm{NaNH}_{2} \text{ / inert solvent}} \mathrm{P}$
options: $\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$
$\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$
$\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{C}\equiv\mathrm{CH}$
No reaction
questions.solution:
The reaction of an internal alkyne with a strong base like $\mathrm{NaNH}_{2}$ at elevated temperatures results in its isomerization to a terminal alkyne. This is known as the alkyne zipper reaction.

The strong base abstracts a proton from the alpha-carbon, forming an allene intermediate ($\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$). Further deprotonation and protonation steps shift the triple bond to the terminal position.

The driving force for this isomerization is the formation of a stable sodium acetylide salt. The terminal alkyne has an acidic hydrogen that is irreversibly abstracted by $\mathrm{NaNH}_{2}$ in the reaction mixture:

$\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{C}\equiv\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{CH}_{3} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{NaNH}_{2}, \Delta} \mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{C}\equiv\mathrm{C}^{-} \mathrm{Na}^{+}$

Upon aqueous workup, the acetylide ion is protonated to yield 1-butyne as the final major organic product:

$\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{C}\equiv\mathrm{C}^{-} \mathrm{Na}^{+} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}} \mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{C}\equiv\mathrm{CH}$

Answer: $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{C}\equiv\mathrm{CH}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Ethylbenzene, when treated with chlorine in the presence of light, mainly gives:
options: $\alpha$-phenylethyl chloride
$o$- and $p$-chloroethylbenzene
$\beta$-phenylethyl chloride
$o$-chloroethylbenzene
questions.solution:
Ethylbenzene reacts with chlorine in the presence of light via a free radical substitution mechanism.

The substitution takes place at the alkyl side chain rather than the aromatic ring.

The intermediate formed is a free radical. The radical formed at the $\alpha$-carbon (benzylic position) is highly stabilized by resonance with the benzene ring, whereas the radical at the $\beta$-carbon is a primary radical and lacks resonance stabilization.

Due to the higher stability of the benzylic free radical, the major product is formed by the substitution of a hydrogen atom at the $\alpha$-carbon.

The resulting product is $\alpha$-phenylethyl chloride ($C_6H_5CHClCH_3$).

Answer: $\alpha$-phenylethyl chloride

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The number of different products obtained upon the complete ozonolysis of 1,2-diethylbenzene is:
questions.solution:
1,2-diethylbenzene exists as a resonance hybrid of two Kekule structures.

In the first Kekule structure, there is a double bond between the carbon atoms bearing the ethyl groups (C1 and C2). Ozonolysis cleaves the C1=C2, C3=C4, and C5=C6 double bonds, yielding one molecule of hexane-3,4-dione ($CH_3CH_2-CO-CO-CH_2CH_3$) and two molecules of glyoxal ($CHO-CHO$).

In the second Kekule structure, there is a single bond between C1 and C2. Ozonolysis cleaves the C2=C3, C4=C5, and C6=C1 double bonds, yielding two molecules of 2-oxobutanal ($CH_3CH_2-CO-CHO$) and one molecule of glyoxal ($CHO-CHO$).

Combining the products from both structures, the complete ozonolysis yields three different products:
1. Glyoxal ($CHO-CHO$)
2. 2-oxobutanal ($CH_3CH_2-CO-CHO$)
3. Hexane-3,4-dione ($CH_3CH_2-CO-CO-CH_2CH_3$)

Thus, the number of different products is 3.

Answer: 3

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the product $\mathrm{P}$ in the given reaction:
options: $\mathrm{C_6H_5CH_2CH_2C_6H_5}$
$\mathrm{C_6H_5CH=CHC_6H_5}$
questions.solution:
The given reactant is benzoin ($\mathrm{C_6H_5-CH(OH)-CO-C_6H_5}$).

The reagent provided is zinc amalgam and concentrated hydrochloric acid ($\mathrm{Zn/Hg}$ and $\mathrm{HCl}$), which is the standard reagent for Clemmensen reduction.

During the Clemmensen reduction of benzoin:
1. The carbonyl group ($>\mathrm{C=O}$) is reduced to a methylene group ($-\mathrm{CH_2}-$).
2. The hydroxyl group ($-\mathrm{OH}$) is at a benzylic position. Under the strongly acidic and reducing conditions of the Clemmensen reduction, benzylic alcohols are also reduced to the corresponding alkanes (via the formation of a resonance-stabilized benzylic carbocation which accepts electrons from zinc).

Therefore, both the ketone and the benzylic alcohol groups are reduced, yielding 1,2-diphenylethane (bibenzyl) as the final product.

Reaction:
$\mathrm{C_6H_5-CH(OH)-CO-C_6H_5} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{Zn/Hg,\ HCl}} \mathrm{C_6H_5-CH_2-CH_2-C_6H_5}$

Answer: $\mathrm{C_6H_5CH_2CH_2C_6H_5}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product for the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reaction is a Friedel-Crafts acylation.

The reactant is anisole (methoxybenzene). The methoxy group ($-OCH_3$) is an electron-donating group due to the $+R$ effect of the oxygen atom, making the benzene ring more reactive towards electrophilic aromatic substitution. It is an ortho/para-directing group.

The reagent is butyryl chloride ($CH_3CH_2CH_2COCl$) in the presence of anhydrous $AlCl_3$ (a Lewis acid). The $AlCl_3$ reacts with butyryl chloride to generate the acylium ion electrophile, $CH_3CH_2CH_2C^+=O$.

The electrophile attacks the anisole ring. Due to the steric hindrance at the ortho position caused by the methoxy group and the bulky nature of the acylium ion, the attack predominantly occurs at the less hindered para position.

Therefore, the major product is formed by the substitution of the para-hydrogen with the butyryl group ($-COCH_2CH_2CH_3$), yielding 1-(4-methoxyphenyl)butan-1-one.

Comparing this with the given options, Option (D) correctly shows the para-substituted product with a four-carbon acyl chain.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the correct order of increasing reactivity for the given aromatic compounds towards electrophilic bromination using $\mathrm{Br}_{2} / \mathrm{FeBr}_{3}$.
questions.solution:
The reactivity of aromatic compounds towards electrophilic aromatic substitution depends on the electron density of the benzene ring.

In phenol, the $-\mathrm{OH}$ group is strongly activating due to its strong $+R$ effect, which dominates over its $-I$ effect. This increases the electron density on the ring, making phenol more reactive than benzene.

In chlorobenzene, the $-\mathrm{Cl}$ group is deactivating because its electron-withdrawing $-I$ effect is stronger than its electron-donating $+R$ effect. This decreases the electron density on the ring, making chlorobenzene less reactive than benzene.

Therefore, the correct order of increasing reactivity towards electrophilic bromination is: Chlorobenzene $<$ Benzene $<$ Phenol.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product A in the following reaction sequence:
options: HCHO
HCOOH
$\mathrm{CH}_{2}(\mathrm{COOH})_{2}$
$\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COOH}$
questions.solution:
The given reactant is 1,4-cyclohexadiene.

Step (i) involves the reaction with $O_3 / H_2O$, which is an oxidative ozonolysis. The carbon-carbon double bonds are cleaved and the resulting aldehyde groups are oxidized to carboxylic acid groups. Cleavage of the two double bonds in 1,4-cyclohexadiene yields two molecules of malonic acid, $CH_2(COOH)_2$.

Step (ii) involves heating the product. Malonic acid is a 1,3-dicarboxylic acid, which is unstable to heat and readily undergoes decarboxylation (loss of $CO_2$).

$HOOC-CH_2-COOH \xrightarrow{\Delta} CH_3COOH + CO_2$

Thus, the final major product A is acetic acid, $CH_3COOH$.

Answer: $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COOH}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the structure of compound P in the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reactant is 3-phenylcyclopropene.

Step (i): Reaction with alkaline $\text{KMnO}_4$ (Baeyer's reagent) leads to the syn-hydroxylation of the carbon-carbon double bond. This converts the alkene into a vicinal diol, forming 3-phenylcyclopropane-1,2-diol.

Step (ii): Reaction with periodic acid ($\text{HIO}_4$) causes the oxidative cleavage of the vicinal diol. The C-C bond between the two hydroxyl-bearing carbons is broken.

Since each of the hydroxyl-bearing carbons in the cyclopropane ring is attached to one hydrogen atom, the cleavage oxidizes both carbons to aldehyde groups ($-\text{CHO}$).

The resulting product is 2-phenylpropanedial, which has the structure $\text{Ph}-\text{CH}(\text{CHO})_2$. This matches the structure given in Option (D).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Which of the following statements is correct regarding the oxidation of the given compounds?
(i)
(ii)
options: Compound (i) is easily oxidizable to benzoic acid, while compound (ii) is oxidizable to benzoic acid only under vigorous conditions.
Compound (i) is oxidizable to benzoic acid, while compound (ii) is oxidizable to 2,2-dimethylpropanoic acid only under vigorous conditions.
Both compounds are oxidizable to benzoic acid under similar conditions.
It is very difficult to oxidize either of the two compounds.
questions.solution:
Compound (i) is cyclopropylbenzene. The carbon atom of the cyclopropyl group attached directly to the benzene ring is a benzylic carbon, and it possesses one benzylic hydrogen. Alkylbenzenes with at least one benzylic hydrogen are readily oxidized by strong oxidizing agents (like acidic or alkaline $KMnO_4$) to form benzoic acid. Thus, cyclopropylbenzene is easily oxidizable to benzoic acid.

Compound (ii) is tert-butylbenzene. The benzylic carbon in this molecule is attached to three methyl groups and has no benzylic hydrogen atoms. Because it lacks benzylic hydrogens, the side chain is highly resistant to oxidation. Under normal oxidizing conditions, it does not react. However, under vigorous oxidation conditions, the benzene ring itself (being more susceptible than the tert-butyl group) undergoes oxidative cleavage, resulting in the formation of 2,2-dimethylpropanoic acid (pivalic acid).

Therefore, compound (i) is oxidizable to benzoic acid, while compound (ii) is oxidizable to 2,2-dimethylpropanoic acid only under vigorous conditions.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds is the least reactive towards the electrophilic addition of $\mathrm{HCl}$?
options: $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{CCH}_{3}$
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CHCHO}$
$\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$
questions.solution:
Electrophilic addition of $\mathrm{HCl}$ involves the attack of an electrophile ($\mathrm{H}^{+}$) on the $\pi$ bond, forming a carbocation intermediate.

The reactivity of the $\pi$ bond towards electrophilic addition depends on its electron density and the stability of the intermediate carbocation.

In $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CHCHO}$, the $-\mathrm{CHO}$ group is a strong electron-withdrawing group due to its $-\mathrm{M}$ and $-\mathrm{I}$ effects. This significantly decreases the electron density on the carbon-carbon double bond, making it least nucleophilic.

Furthermore, the carbocation formed upon protonation would be highly destabilized by the adjacent electron-withdrawing carbonyl group.

Therefore, $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CHCHO}$ is the least reactive towards the electrophilic addition of $\mathrm{HCl}$.

Answer: $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CHCHO}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the most suitable reaction sequence to carry out the following transformation.
options: $\xrightarrow[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}]{\mathrm{HNO}_{3}} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{NBS}}$
$\xrightarrow[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}]{\mathrm{HNO}_{3}} \xrightarrow[\mathrm{FeBr}_{3}]{\mathrm{Br}_{2}}$
$\xrightarrow[\mathrm{HCl}]{\mathrm{NaNO}_{2}} \xrightarrow[\mathrm{FeBr}_{3}]{\mathrm{Br}_{2}}$
$\xrightarrow[\mathrm{FeBr}_{3}]{\mathrm{Br}_{2}} \xrightarrow[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}]{\mathrm{HNO}_{3}}$
questions.solution:
The transformation requires the synthesis of p-bromonitrobenzene from benzene.

The two substituents on the benzene ring are $-\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ and $-\mathrm{Br}$.

The $-\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ group is a meta-directing group, whereas the $-\mathrm{Br}$ group is an ortho/para-directing group.

If nitration is performed first, the intermediate is nitrobenzene. Subsequent bromination will direct the incoming bromine atom to the meta position, yielding m-bromonitrobenzene.

If bromination is performed first, the intermediate is bromobenzene. Subsequent nitration will direct the incoming nitro group to the ortho and para positions. Due to steric hindrance, the para isomer (p-bromonitrobenzene) will be the major product.

Therefore, the correct sequence of reactions is bromination followed by nitration.

Step 1: Bromination of benzene using $\mathrm{Br}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{FeBr}_{3}$ to form bromobenzene.

Step 2: Nitration of bromobenzene using concentrated $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ and concentrated $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}$ to form p-bromonitrobenzene as the major product.

Answer: $\xrightarrow[\mathrm{FeBr}_{3}]{\mathrm{Br}_{2}} \xrightarrow[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}]{\mathrm{HNO}_{3}}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the major product $\mathrm{Z}$ for the following reaction:

$+\ \mathrm{HBr} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Z}$
questions.solution:
The given reactant is a phenol derivative with a conjugated alkene side chain (a 1-alkenyl group).

The reaction involves the electrophilic addition of $\mathrm{HBr}$ to the alkene double bond.

Protonation of the double bond can generate two possible carbocation intermediates:
1. Addition of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ to the $\beta$-carbon forms a benzylic carbocation at the $\alpha$-carbon. This intermediate is highly stabilized by resonance with the benzene ring, which is further enhanced by the strong $+\mathrm{R}$ (electron-donating) effect of the $-\mathrm{OH}$ group at the para position.
2. Addition of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ to the $\alpha$-carbon forms a secondary carbocation, which lacks this resonance stabilization.

Because the benzylic carbocation is significantly more stable, the reaction proceeds almost exclusively via this intermediate.

Subsequent nucleophilic attack by the bromide ion ($\mathrm{Br}^{-}$) on the benzylic carbocation yields the major product, where the bromine atom is attached to the benzylic position.

The phenolic $-\mathrm{OH}$ group remains unreacted because the aryl $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{O}$ bond has partial double bond character and does not easily undergo nucleophilic substitution with $\mathrm{HBr}$.

Option (A) correctly shows the product with the bromine atom at the benzylic position and the $-\mathrm{OH}$ group intact.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the following two reaction sequences:

(I)

(II)

Which of the following correctly compares the overall rates of these two reactions?
options: Rate of (I) $=$ Rate of (II)
Rate of (I) < Rate of (II)
Rate of (I) > Rate of (II)
Cannot be predicted from the given information
questions.solution:
The given reaction sequence involves the nitration of benzene followed by the reduction of the nitro group.

In the nitration of benzene, the rate-determining step (RDS) is the attack of the electrophile ($NO_2^+$) on the aromatic ring to form the sigma complex (arenium ion).

The breaking of the C-H or C-D bond occurs in the subsequent fast step to restore aromaticity. Since the cleavage of the C-H or C-D bond is not involved in the rate-determining step, there is no primary kinetic isotope effect ($k_H / k_D \approx 1$).

Thus, the rate of nitration of benzene ($C_6H_6$) is equal to the rate of nitration of hexadeuterobenzene ($C_6D_6$).

The subsequent reduction of the $-NO_2$ group to $-NH_2$ does not involve the cleavage of ring C-H or C-D bonds, so its rate is also unaffected by the isotopic substitution.

Therefore, the overall rate of reaction (I) is equal to the overall rate of reaction (II).

Answer: Rate of (I) $=$ Rate of (II)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Ethylene can be prepared in good yield by which of the following reactions?
options: Both the reactions in B and C
$\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{NH}_{2} \xrightarrow{\text{Heat}} \mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}_{2} + \mathrm{NH}_{3}$

$\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{N}^{+}(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{3}\mathrm{OH}^{-} \xrightarrow{\text{Heat}} \mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}_{2} + (\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{3}\mathrm{N} + \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$
$\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{N}^{+}(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{3}\mathrm{I}^{-} \xrightarrow{\text{Heat}} \mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}_{2} + (\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{3}\mathrm{N} + \mathrm{HI}$
questions.solution:
The preparation of ethylene in good yield can be achieved by the Hofmann elimination reaction.

When a quaternary ammonium hydroxide is heated, it undergoes an E2 elimination to form an alkene, a tertiary amine, and water. The hydroxide ion acts as a strong base, abstracting a $\beta$-hydrogen atom.

$\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{N}^{+}(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{3}\mathrm{OH}^{-} \xrightarrow{\text{Heat}} \mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}_{2} + (\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{3}\mathrm{N} + \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$

Primary amines (as in option B) do not undergo elimination simply upon heating. Quaternary ammonium iodides (as in option D) undergo nucleophilic substitution rather than elimination upon heating, because the iodide ion is a weak base and a good nucleophile.

Answer: $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{N}^{+}(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{3}\mathrm{OH}^{-} \xrightarrow{\text{Heat}} \mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}_{2} + (\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{3}\mathrm{N} + \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the product Z in the following reaction:
options: Both B and C
$\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{CCOOH}$
questions.solution:
The given reactant is p-tert-butyltoluene, which contains a methyl group and a tert-butyl group attached to the benzene ring.

When treated with a strong oxidizing agent like $\mathrm{K_2Cr_2O_7}$ in the presence of $\mathrm{H_2SO_4}$, alkyl groups attached to a benzene ring are oxidized to a carboxylic acid ($-\mathrm{COOH}$) group, provided they have at least one benzylic hydrogen atom.

The methyl group ($-\mathrm{CH_3}$) has three benzylic hydrogens and is therefore oxidized to a $-\mathrm{COOH}$ group.

The tert-butyl group ($-\mathrm{C(CH_3)_3}$) has no benzylic hydrogens and is resistant to oxidation under these conditions.

Therefore, the product Z is p-tert-butylbenzoic acid, which corresponds to the structure in option (B).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following compounds in decreasing order of their reactivity towards electrophilic aromatic substitution.

(I)
(II)
(III)
options: $\text{III} > \text{I} > \text{II}$
$\text{III} > \text{II} > \text{I}$
$\text{I} > \text{II} > \text{III}$
$\text{I} > \text{II} = \text{III}$
questions.solution:
The given compounds are (I) Benzene, (II) Pyridine, and (III) Pyrrole.

The reactivity of an aromatic compound towards electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS) depends on the electron density of the ring. Higher electron density makes the ring more nucleophilic and thus more reactive towards electrophiles.

In pyrrole (III), the lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom is delocalized into the five-membered ring to maintain aromaticity (6 $\pi$ electrons). This $+M$ effect significantly increases the electron density on the carbon atoms of the ring, making pyrrole highly activated and most reactive towards EAS.

In pyridine (II), the nitrogen atom is $sp^2$ hybridized and its lone pair does not participate in the aromatic $\pi$ system. Nitrogen is more electronegative than carbon, so it withdraws electron density from the ring via the $-I$ effect. Additionally, resonance structures of pyridine place a positive charge on the ortho and para positions, making the ring electron-deficient. Thus, pyridine is highly deactivated and least reactive towards EAS.

Benzene (I) serves as the standard reference. It is less reactive than the electron-rich pyrrole but much more reactive than the electron-deficient pyridine.

Therefore, the decreasing order of reactivity towards electrophilic aromatic substitution is Pyrrole (III) > Benzene (I) > Pyridine (II).

Answer: $\text{III} > \text{I} > \text{II}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction sequence.
questions.solution:
Reaction of cyclohexene with $O_3 / H_2O_2$ (oxidative ozonolysis) cleaves the double bond to form adipic acid.

Cyclohexene $\rightarrow$ $HOOC-(CH_2)_4-COOH$

Adipic acid undergoes esterification with excess $MeOH / H^+$ to form dimethyl adipate.

$HOOC-(CH_2)_4-COOH \rightarrow MeOOC-(CH_2)_4-COOMe$

Dimethyl adipate undergoes Dieckmann condensation (intramolecular Claisen condensation) in the presence of the base $NaOMe$.

The methoxide ion abstracts an $\alpha$-proton from one ester group to form an enolate.

The enolate nucleophilically attacks the carbonyl carbon of the second ester group, forming a cyclic intermediate.

Loss of a methoxide ion yields a stable five-membered ring.

The final major product is a cyclic $\beta$-keto ester, methyl 2-oxocyclopentanecarboxylate, which corresponds to the structure in option (4).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the major product of the following reaction sequence:

$\xrightarrow[\substack{\text{(iii) } \mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{Et}_2\mathrm{O} \ \text{(iv) } \mathrm{CO}_2 \text{, then } \mathrm{H}^+}]{\substack{\text{(i) } \mathrm{Br}_2 / \mathrm{Fe} \ \text{(ii) } \mathrm{K}_2\mathrm{Cr}_2\mathrm{O}_7 / \mathrm{H}^+ / \Delta}}$ Product
questions.solution:
Step (i): Toluene undergoes electrophilic aromatic substitution with $\mathrm{Br}_2/\mathrm{Fe}$. The $-\mathrm{CH}_3$ group is ortho/para directing. The major product is p-bromotoluene due to steric hindrance at the ortho position.

Step (ii): Oxidation of p-bromotoluene with $\mathrm{K}_2\mathrm{Cr}_2\mathrm{O}_7/\mathrm{H}^+$ converts the benzylic $-\mathrm{CH}_3$ group to a $-\mathrm{COOH}$ group, yielding p-bromobenzoic acid.

Step (iii): Reaction of p-bromobenzoic acid with $\mathrm{Li}/\mathrm{Et}_2\mathrm{O}$ first deprotonates the acidic $-\mathrm{COOH}$ group to form a lithium carboxylate salt ($-\mathrm{COOLi}$). Excess lithium then reacts with the $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Br}$ bond to form an aryllithium species, resulting in the intermediate lithium p-lithiobenzoate.

Step (iv): The aryllithium intermediate reacts with $\mathrm{CO}_2$ to form a second carboxylate group. Subsequent acidic workup ($\mathrm{H}^+$) protonates both carboxylate groups to yield benzene-1,4-dicarboxylic acid (terephthalic acid).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Pyrolysis of sec-butyl acetate ($\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COOCH}(\mathrm{CH}_{3})\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{3}$) gives:
options: 1-butene and 2-butene in a 1:2 molar ratio
1-butene and 2-butene in a 2:3 molar ratio
1-butene and 2-butene in an equimolar ratio
1-butene and 2-butene in a 3:2 molar ratio
questions.solution:
Pyrolysis of esters is a syn-elimination reaction that proceeds via a six-membered cyclic transition state.

The direction of elimination is primarily governed by the statistical availability of $\beta$-hydrogens because the activation energies for the abstraction of different $\beta$-hydrogens are very similar in this intramolecular process.

In $sec$-butyl acetate, the acetate group is attached to the second carbon atom. The $\beta$-hydrogens are located on C1 and C3.

The number of $\beta$-hydrogens on C1 is 3. Elimination involving any of these yields 1-butene.

The number of $\beta$-hydrogens on C3 is 2. Elimination involving any of these yields 2-butene.

Since the elimination is statistically controlled, the molar ratio of 1-butene to 2-butene formed is equal to the ratio of the number of available $\beta$-hydrogens.

Ratio of 1-butene to 2-butene = 3:2.

Answer: 1-butene and 2-butene in a 3:2 molar ratio

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given starting material is a tetracyclic compound containing two cyclobutene rings fused to a central octalin-like system with four allylic bromine atoms. The reaction conditions involve heating ($\Delta$) and sodium iodide (NaI) in acetone.

First, under thermal conditions, the cyclobutene rings undergo electrocyclic ring opening. The single bond opposite to the double bond in each cyclobutene ring breaks, expanding the fused 6-membered rings into 8-membered rings. This thermal ring opening of the two cyclobutene moieties yields a bicyclic system with two fused 8-membered rings, specifically a 1,6-dibromo-2,4-hexadiene system on both sides of the central double bond.

Next, the addition of 2 equivalents of NaI (which is sufficient to dehalogenate both sides, as 1 eq. of NaI per dibromide pair is often implied in such stoichiometric notations for double eliminations, or it simply drives the full dehalogenation) promotes a 1,6-elimination of the bromine atoms.

The iodide ion attacks the allylic bromines, leading to the elimination of Br$_2$ (as I$_2$ and NaBr) and the rearrangement of the double bonds to form a fully conjugated system. The 1,6-elimination on both the left and right 8-membered rings results in the formation of three conjugated double bonds in each ring, while preserving the central double bond.

The final product is octalene, a bicyclic fully conjugated hydrocarbon consisting of two fused cyclooctatetraene rings. This matches the structure shown in Option (D).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reaction is an example of the Ramberg-Bäcklund reaction, which converts $\alpha$-halo sulfones into alkenes in the presence of a strong base.

In the presence of $\text{NaOD}$ and $\text{D}_2\text{O}$, the acidic protons at the $\alpha$-positions (adjacent to the electron-withdrawing $-\text{SO}_2-$ group) undergo rapid hydrogen-deuterium (H/D) exchange before the main reaction proceeds. The $-\text{CH}_2-$ group is converted to $-\text{CD}_2-$ and the $-\text{CH(Cl)}-$ group is converted to $-\text{CD(Cl)}-$.

The base ($\text{OD}^-$) then abstracts a deuterium ion from the $-\text{CD}_2-$ group to form a carbanion. This carbanion undergoes an intramolecular nucleophilic substitution, attacking the adjacent $-\text{CD(Cl)}-$ carbon and displacing the chloride ion ($\text{Cl}^-$) to form a strained three-membered episulfone (thiirane 1,1-dioxide) intermediate.

Finally, the episulfone intermediate undergoes a cheletropic elimination of sulfur dioxide ($\text{SO}_2$) to yield the corresponding alkene.

The resulting product is 9,10-dideuterophenanthrene, which corresponds to the structure in option (B).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction sequence:
questions.solution:
The reaction of 3-methylbut-1-yne with LDA, a strong base, results in the deprotonation of the terminal alkyne to form an acetylide ion.

$(CH_3)_2CH-C \equiv CH \xrightarrow{LDA} (CH_3)_2CH-C \equiv C^-$

The acetylide ion then acts as a nucleophile and undergoes an $S_N2$ reaction with 1-bromopentane, yielding the internal alkyne 2-methylnon-3-yne.

$(CH_3)_2CH-C \equiv C^- + CH_3(CH_2)_4Br \rightarrow (CH_3)_2CH-C \equiv C-(CH_2)_4CH_3$

Next, hydroboration of this internal alkyne with $BH_3 \cdot THF$ takes place. The addition of borane across the triple bond is a syn addition. The regioselectivity is determined by steric hindrance. Since the isopropyl group is more sterically demanding than the straight-chain n-pentyl group, the boron atom preferentially attacks the less hindered carbon atom (the one attached to the n-pentyl group). The hydrogen atom adds to the more hindered carbon atom (attached to the isopropyl group).

This step produces a cis-alkenylborane intermediate.

Finally, the intermediate is treated with $CH_3COOD$ (MeCOOD). The deuteronolysis of the carbon-boron bond proceeds with complete retention of configuration. The deuterium atom replaces the boron group exactly where it was.

The resulting major product is a cis-alkene where the hydrogen atom is on the carbon bearing the isopropyl group, and the deuterium atom is on the carbon bearing the n-pentyl group. The isopropyl and n-pentyl groups are cis to each other.

This structure matches the one given in option (D).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The reactivity and regioselectivity of pyridine towards electrophilic aromatic substitution most closely resemble those of:
options: none of these
aniline
benzene
nitrobenzene
questions.solution:
Pyridine is a heterocyclic aromatic compound where the nitrogen atom is more electronegative than the carbon atoms. This causes the nitrogen to withdraw electron density from the ring through both the inductive effect ($-I$) and the resonance effect ($-R$).

Due to this electron withdrawal, the pyridine ring is highly deactivated towards electrophilic aromatic substitution, similar to a benzene ring with a strongly electron-withdrawing group.

When an electrophile attacks pyridine, it preferentially attacks at the meta position (C-3 or C-5). Attack at the ortho or para positions leads to a highly unstable resonance structure where the positive charge resides on the electronegative nitrogen atom with an incomplete octet.

Nitrobenzene also has a strongly electron-withdrawing nitro group, making the ring highly deactivated towards electrophilic aromatic substitution and directing the incoming electrophile to the meta position.

Therefore, both the reactivity (highly deactivated) and regioselectivity (meta-directing) of pyridine most closely resemble those of nitrobenzene.

Answer: nitrobenzene

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction sequence:
questions.solution:
Step 1: The reaction of benzene with acetyl chloride ($\text{CH}_3\text{COCl}$) in the presence of anhydrous $\text{AlCl}_3$ is a Friedel-Crafts acylation. This introduces an acetyl group onto the benzene ring, forming acetophenone ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COCH}_3$).

Step 2: Acetophenone is then treated with zinc amalgam ($\text{Zn-Hg}$) and concentrated $\text{HCl}$. This is the Clemmensen reduction, which reduces the carbonyl group ($>\text{C=O}$) to a methylene group ($>\text{CH}_2$), converting acetophenone into ethylbenzene ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_3$).

Step 3: Ethylbenzene is reacted with bromine ($\text{Br}_2$) in the presence of sunlight ($h\nu$). This initiates a free radical substitution reaction. The substitution occurs preferentially at the benzylic position because the intermediate benzylic free radical is highly stabilized by resonance with the benzene ring. Thus, the major product is 1-bromo-1-phenylethane ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{CH(Br)CH}_3$).

Answer:
